issue_title: Roadmap for 1.0 release?
issue_body:
The library seems quite usable. I think 1.0 releases are a good way to communicate that things are stable and working.

I realize the TOML standard itself is not at 1.0 (toml-lang/toml#482) but as the issue there says, 0.4 is de facto 1.0 because Tom doesn't want to break compatibility.

Anyway, given the standard that we have, is there anything you feel is lacking for a 1.0 release?

issue_title: 调整词典不生效
issue_body:
如下所示

```
>>> print('/'.join(jieba.cut('价格便宜')))
价格便宜
```
默认字典中是这样定义的 确实存在一个价格便宜
```
cat dict.txt | grep -e '^价格 ' -e '^便宜 ' -e '价格便宜'
价格 11762 n
价格便宜 4 n
便宜 2013 a
```
而我想要分成： `价格/便宜` 参照调整词典中的介绍 试了下面的方法 均不成功

```
# 删除掉价格便宜 还是一个整体
>>> jieba.del_word('价格便宜')
>>> print('/'.join(jieba.cut('价格便宜')))
价格便宜

# 调节词频 也不起作用
>>> jieba.suggest_freq(('价格', '便宜'), True)
0
>>> print('/'.join(jieba.cut('价格便宜')))
价格便宜
```

所以说现在不知道该怎么做 才能使得正确拆分？

类似的还有`反应速度快` ==》`反应/速度快`
```
>>> print('/'.join(jieba.cut('反应速度快')))
反应速度快
```

issue_title: I don't like run(shutdown=True)
issue_body:
I'm totally into the "we only have one method" aesthetic but I think maybe it's gone too far :-)

Or maybe it's just that I don't understand what the difference is between `kernel.run(coro, shutdown=False)` and `kernel.run(coro, shutdown=True)`. The docs say "If shutdown is True, the kernel will cancel all daemonic tasks and perform a clean shutdown once all regular tasks have completed." But isn't that, like... what you always want?

But I'm confident that in the `kernel.run(shutdown=False)` version, calling `kernel.run` when what I want is for the kernel to not run, is super confusing. Surely this should be `kernel.shutdown()`.

Or `await curio.shutdown()` for that matter. I don't know why this is the one function in curio that doesn't go through the trap system.

issue_title: Pandas support using new API
issue_body:
The most recent pandas release (0.23) introduces [extension types](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.23/extending.html#extending-extension-types) which I believe provide a natural way to integrate pint types into pandas dataframes on a per row/column basis. Is this a feature that could be merged here (assuming no breakage when older/no pandas is installed)?
If so, I'd be interested in researching that possibility further and maybe even implementing it myself. As a disclaimer, I've only started using pint quite recently and am not very proficient with pandas either and I'm not sure when I really get the time to do this. In any case, if someone else beats me to coding the feature, that'd be perfect, too :)

Related issues (which I _haven't_ read fully):
- #401 
- https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/10349,
- an early user of the new API is [cyberpandas](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/cyberpandas)

issue_title: Example submission
issue_body:
Recursive upload:
```
import os
import yadisk


y = yadisk.YaDisk("<id-приложения>", "<secret-приложения>", "<токен>")
to_dir = '/test'
from_dir = '/home/ubuntu'
for root, dirs, files in os.walk(from_dir):
    p = root.split(from_dir)[1]
    if not y.exists(to_dir + p):
        y.mkdir(to_dir + p)
    for file in files:
        if not y.exists(to_dir + p + '/' + file):
            y.upload(from_dir + p + '/' + file, to_dir + p + '/' + file)
```

issue_title: Improve talkback regex
issue_body:
Currently the talkback plugin only responds if a user types `that's what she said`.

Many people are lazy, however, so the majority will probably say `thats what she said`.

I propose this regex:
`that('s|s) what she said`
or alternatively:
`that(.s|s) what she said`

issue_title: Implement option to ignore deleted files
issue_body: 99% of the time when I'm doing a diff I am not interested in files that have been deleted. So it would be nice if cdiff had an option to suppress the inclusion of such files in its output. The obvious way to do this is to utilize `git diff --irreversible-delete` (which is what I normally run when I'm not using `cdiff`). The other solution is to use `git diff --diff-filter=d` which is similar but also omits the diff headers for the deleted files.

issue_title: Wrong links get displayed when typing "howdoi cat --all -n 3"
issue_body:
```
>howdoi cat --all -n 3
★  Answer from http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B000RIA95G ★
While cat does stand for "concatenate", what it actually does is simply display one or multiple files, in order of their appearance in the command line arguments to cat . The common pattern to view the contents of a file on Linux or *nix systems is:
cat <file>
The main difference between cat and Git's cat-file is that it only displays a single file (hence the -file part). Git's cat-file doesn't really stand for "concatenate"; it simply is a reference to the behavior of the cat command.
git-cat-file - Provide content or type and size information for repository objects
Technically, you can use git cat-file to concatenate files, if you use Batch Output mode:
BATCH OUTPUT If --batch or --batch-check is given, cat-file will read objects from stdin, one per line, and print information about them. By default, the whole line is considered as an object, as if it were fed to git-rev-parse [1].

★  Answer from http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/0679449531 ★
the problem is that you are using a Fileconnection in combination with cat then the append won't work. There are several option you could use, the most easy one is to this: first "create" the file, if you want to add a header for example:
header = "some header" 
## if you don't want to use a header then leave the header blank
header =""
cat(text, file="outputR.txt", append=FALSE, sep = "\n")
notice the append = FALSE this is necessary if you want to clear the file if it already exist otherwise you have to use append = TRUE the you can write text to it using:
text = text = paste(toString(id), ":", toString(goIDs))
cat(text file="outputR.txt", append=TRUE, sep = "\n")

★  Answer from http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B003E6WSHK ★
$ cat > hello.c
#include <stdio.h>

main(void) {
    printf("Hello, world.\n");
}
^D
$ gcc hello.c
$ ./a.out
Hello, world.
```

If I request the actual links though...
```
>howdoi cat -l -n 3
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38177026/what-does-cat-file-stand-for-in-git

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39942297/r-doesnt-append-lines-to-file-using-cat-or-write

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/921455/how-do-i-program-using-cat
```

issue_title: My LDAP server connection gets disconnected every 5 minutes. How to automatically reconnect?
Recommendations for using connection pooling with frequent rebinds.
issue_body:
Hi,

This is just a question so hopefully this is the correct place to submit it.

We are looking for recommendations to minimize our open connections to LDAP. Our current configuration involves using pooled connections bound with a service account to make numerous calls to access data contained in our LDAP server. Additionally, we also use the LDAP server to authenticate users. To authenticate users we establish a new connection and attempt to bind with that users uname and password. This current flow causes problems because it establishes many connections to the LDAP server and we would rather utilize the pool since SSL handshakes and other TCP transactions have already been established. One idea is to rebind the pooled connection with the new user report success and then rebind back to the service account.

When reviewing the code rebinding on a pooled connection will be difficult because of two main hurdles.

- When calling rebind on a reusable pooled connection the code iterates over each worker and rebinds with the new user and password. This seems dangerous in our situation since all the connections in the pool will no longer be associated with the service account originally setup and now be bound to the user and password for the one authentication attempt. The fear is in high volume situations requests may come in before rebinding may occur.

- Rebind also appears to be lazy loaded by design which is great feature except we need to rely on a subsequent call to get the results of the bind. This feels problematic in high volume pooled instances.

Thanks for reviewing. Have you encountered any similar flows with the library? Do you have any recommendations to handle our use case?

issue_title: Slow performance of  'region' and 'children' methods
issue_body:
Hi Daler,
First of all thanks for gffutils – crucial thing for every genomics biologist using Python : - ))

I've been recently running some scripts. The performance of them is quite slow and I think there some simple changes to sqlite queries which can speed things up dramatically.
1. region method actually make a query on seqid, start, end. So I manually created multicolumn index (seqid, start, end) on table features. After that everything has become 10x or may be even more quicker. Maybe this index can be added on the initial DB creation since it is so important for region method?
2. children method with featuretype keyword runs slow. When I tried the actual query which happens behind the scenes and made EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN on it it returns
0|0|0|SEARCH TABLE features USING INDEX featuretype (featuretype=?)
0|1|1|SEARCH TABLE relations USING COVERING INDEX sqlite_autoindex_relations_1 (parent=? AND child=?)
The thing is that there are a lot of features of certain type (which you indicate as a featuretype keyword) and then this whole bunch (in case of exon almost the whole table) is intersected with what relations table return. If this is done vice verse i.e. first the relation table is searched and then the featuretype is checked then the performance is orders of magnitude faster.

All the best,
Mikhail

issue_title: JWT strings different between python 2 and 3
issue_body:
Because python dictionaries arent ordered by default in 2 and are ordered by default in 3, pyjwt generates different jwt strings because of https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/master/jwt/api_jws.py#L83. This affects applications and libs that are doing jwt comparisons.

Would it be acceptable to change that to use an OrderedDict instead? That way, the jwt strings will be the same between 2 and 3.

Please clear my doubts as I am totally bugged with the use of sid.

issue_title: Suggestion: clarify this is a Python 3 package
issue_body:
Since this package targets users that might have limited knowledge about Python versions it might be a good idea to clearly indicate on the README that this is a Python 3 only package.

I would probably go as far as to add this to the setup.py file:

```python
from sys import version_info
if version_info.major != 3:
    print("This package will only work with Python 3. \n" 
          "If you already have Python 3 installed try 'pip3 install guizero'.")
```

This way it will not break with cryptic messages during installation or first run.

issue_title: Comment support for parser
issue_body: Currently, there seem to be two different lexers: the normal one and a full one (which preserves comments for example). There is only a normal parser, so I would have to use the full lexer if I want to work with the comments later. Having a full parser which supports comments would be easier to use. (Using `parser.parse(infile.read(), lexer=full_lexer.clone())` throws an SyntaxError on `<?php` which does not occur when calling a `full_lexer.clone()` instance directly without the parser.)

issue_title: "Exception: No domain found" if there are more than 100 domains in AWS Route53 account
issue_body:
We have more than 100 domains in our AWS Route53 account. From [docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/route53/list-hosted-zones-by-name.html): "Amazon Route 53 returns up to 100 items in each response." 

Trying to run this on a domain which is not included in the first 100 listed domains fails with "Exception: No domain found" error message:
```
# lexicon route53 --auth-access-key="KEY" --auth-access-secret="SECRET" --name "_test.testdomain.com" --content "XYZ" create testdomain.com TXT
```

Also from docs: "If the value of IsTruncated in the response is true, there are more hosted zones associated with the current AWS account. If IsTruncated is false, this response includes the last hosted zone that is associated with the current account. The NextDNSName element and NextHostedZoneId elements are omitted from the response."

I guess it should check for "IsTruncated" in response and act accordingly.

issue_title: Column slicing issues
issue_body:
When attempting the following code
```
import fitsio
f = fitsio.FITS('spec-3874-55280-0067.fits')
clolumn = f[2]['SUBCLASS']
```
column is returned as
```
file: ~/spec-3874-55280-0067.fits
  extension: 2
  type: BINARY_TBL
  rows: 1
  column subset:
    CLASS               S6  array[6]
    SUBCLASS           S21  array[21]
    U                   f4  array[5]
    L                   f8  
    B                   f8  
```
and attempting to take column[:] crashes iPython.

If I use
```
columns = f[2]['CLASS','SUBCLASS']
```
returns
```
file: ~/spec-3874-55280-0067.fits
  extension: 2
  type: BINARY_TBL
  rows: 1
  column subset:
    CLASS               S6  array[6]
    SUBCLASS           S21  array[21]
```
and columns[:] works fine in this case.

It looks like when specifying a single column, fitsio searches for any subset of the given column name.

issue_title: Automatically generate pheno data
Switch CLI API dispatch framework to `docopt_dispatch`
issue_body:
The current [command dispatch code](https://github.com/guyzmo/git-repo/blob/devel/git_repo/kwargparse.py) has been designed to avoid an over-complex main function. But it was after coding it that I discovered that the author of docopt implemented a similar framework he called [`docopt_dispatch`](https://github.com/keleshev/docopt-dispatch).

That framework might need some adjustments for custom features built in the current command dispatch code, but it's worth using an existing and common framework than reinventing the wheel.

issue_title: Feature Request

Have you thought of adding a "reload" method to the Netmiko library?  For example, "net_connect.reload()", which would send a "reload" command down the channel, expecting this prompt:

issue_title: trailing slashes for "leaf" directories, usage of '\\'
issue_body:
Hi,

I Wanted to ask regarding some parsing issues I'm having. 

Zipfile listings look like this:
'Folder/Folder2/FILE'
'Folder/Folder2/EmptyFolder/'

With Rarfile it's:
u'Folder\\Folder2\\FILE'
u'Folder\\Folder2\\EmptyFolder'

This causes obstacles - 
1. With the lack of trailing slashes, I need extra info to know if FILE a file or a folder.
2. I would need to parse windows and linux rars differently, as well as rarfiles and zipfiles, gzipfiles etc.

Would it be possible to add trailing slashes to last-in-line folders, and/or use the generic platform agnostic '/' instead of '\\'? That would be awesome, as it would allow to utilize a smooth generic archive parsing.

issue_title: pynlpir.open inline documentation and sphinx documentation missing ValueError
issue_body:
`pynlpir.open`'s inline documentation is missing any note about what a `ValueError` means when it is raised.

The [Sphinx documentation](http://pynlpir.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#pynlpir.open) incorrectly describes the `ValueError` (what it describes is `RuntimeError`). Its  '`ValueError`' should be renamed `'RuntimeError`' and the correct `ValueError` description should be added, just like in the inline documentation.

issue_title: In classes using typing generics, it seems parsing parent classes does not work
issue_body:
See the following example:

```python
b = """
import typing

T = typing.TypeVar('T')

class Foo(typing.Generic[T]):
    def __init__(self, foo: T) -> None:
        self.foo = foo

class Bar(Foo[int]):
    def __init__(self, foo: int, bar: typing.Any) -> None:
        super().__init__(foo=foo)

        self.bar = bar

b = Bar()
b."""

completions = jedi.api.Script(source=b).completions()

print(completions)
```

I would expect that among completions there are both `bar` and `foo`, but it is just `bar`.

I I remove `typing.Generic` from the base class and corresponding code, then it works.

issue_title: PEP8 checking
issue_body:
Hello,

Your code need some cleanup to respect [PEP8](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/).
I suggest to use [flake8](http://flake8.pycqa.org/) for lint.

```
flake8 --ignore E501 imutils demos
```

This can be run on CI server (Travis...) [just after running unit tests](https://github.com/jrosebr1/imutils/issues/14)

Kind regards

```python
import colorama

issue_title: Big changes to how visvis renders the scene
issue_body:
```
I'm considering making some big changes to how visvis renders stuff.

I'm searching for an approach that enables:
  - anti aliasing of any object (lines, meshes, etc.) (full screen anti-aliasing)
  - making screenshots at a higher resolution than screen resolution
  - proper handling of transparency in a generic manner
  - beautiful text
  - preferably allows off line rendering
  - preferably allows objects to render at a lower screen resolution (e.g. faster 3D volume rendering during user interaction)

Two base techniques
-------------------

I've played with two techniques that can serve as a basis for this. The first 
is using the accumulation buffer. Basically, you render the same scene multiple 
times with small offsets in screen resolution (i.e. jitter), and you average 
the result. I got a proof of concept working and the result looks very nice. 
Although it's pretty straight forward, it does seem a bit jerky, probably 
because the whole overhead of the visivis render pipeline is executed multiple 
times.

The second approach uses frame buffer objects (FBO) to render to a texture 
instead of the default frame buffer. You can make this buffer any size you 
want. To do anti-aliasing you simply render the screen at a higher resolution 
and then draw the resulting texture to the screen while averaging the 
"sub-pixels" (in a fragment shader). This approach offers many niceties such as 
high res screenshots, and has the significant advantage that you only have to 
execute the visvis pipeline once.

FBO's are available on many systems as an extension, and are part of the core 
of openGl in 3.0. For systems that do not have FBO's available we can either 
use the accumulation buffer approach, or disable some features.

Transparency
------------

Transparency is hard. To do it right you have to draw the objects in the right 
order, or use approximations to avoid that. In any case you must draw all 
opaque objects first. Two interesting approaches for alpha blending are 
described in 
http://developer.download.nvidia.com/SDK/10/opengl/src/dual_depth_peeling/doc/Du
alDepthPeeling.pdf
One is easy and fast but makes approximations, the other seems harder to 
implement and is slower but provides correct results.

Interestingly, the FBO approach allows us to render each object to a texture 
and then combine these resulting textures using either alpha compositing 
technique. We would thus have a method to deal with transparency in a uniform 
way. (this technique would be per-object though, it would not blend different 
parts of the same mesh).

Text
----

Either approach would make it pretty straightforward to display nice text; we 
simply render the un-anti-aliased text at the higher resolution, and the full 
screen anti-aliasing will make it look nice. 

I tested that this works using text rendered by Qt. The text suffers from the 
standard glitches that anti-aliased text has, such as a bit blurry appearance. 
But rotating text etc. requires nothing special. The FBO approach would 
probably allow rendering text separately on a screen-size texture, using 
sub-pixel positioning to create that ultra-crisp fonts Robert demonstrated in 
issue 25.


```

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `almar.klein@gmail.com` on 20 Jun 2012 at 12:48

issue_title: KafkaConsumer.poll()  never receives first message on a topic
issue_body:
When the consumer is initialized without a group id it can never receive the first message written to a topic.

```
from __future__ import absolute_import, division, print_function
from kafka import KafkaConsumer, KafkaProducer
import time

timeout = 100
topic = 'test.10'

consumer = KafkaConsumer(topic, group_id=None, bootstrap_servers='localhost:9093')
producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers='localhost:9093')

def poll():
    partitions = consumer.poll(timeout)
    if len(partitions) > 0:
        for p in partitions:
            for response in partitions[p]:
                print(response)
    else:
        print("poll() - no results found")            

def produce():
    result = producer.send(topic, 'hello')
    md = result.get()
    print("send() - %s" % str(md))
    
poll()  # set high water mark
produce()
poll()  # get the value
poll()  # try again, just for the heck of it
produce()
poll()  # second produce result is found

---------------
bash$ python /tmp/consumer.py 
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=0)
poll() - no results found
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=1)
ConsumerRecord(topic=u'test.10', partition=0, offset=1, key=None, value='hello')

bash$ python /tmp/consumer.py 
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=2)
ConsumerRecord(topic=u'test.10', partition=0, offset=2, key=None, value='hello')
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=3)
ConsumerRecord(topic=u'test.10', partition=0, offset=3, key=None, value='hello')
```

First run, when before the topic was created the consumer does not receive the first message, but receives messages thereafter.

Second run the first poll() sets the high water mark and subsequent records are found.

Setting the topic to a new topic causes the above results to be repeatable.

issue_title: Manual orders order status
issue_body:
The IB API allows to forward messages related to manual orders to a designated client, identified by the "Master API client ID" in TWS (or IB gateway) settings.  When the ib_insync client is identified as the designated client, the messages received are not correctly recognized, I see the following error reported

ERROR:ib_insync.wrapper:orderStatus: No order found for orderId 0 and clientId 0
ERROR:ib_insync.wrapper:orderStatus: No order found for orderId 0 and clientId 0
ERROR:ib_insync.wrapper:orderStatus: No order found for orderId 0 and clientId 0

There are multiple errors because there are multiple status messages for each order.  Surprisingly all orders seem also tagged with the same orderId, i.e. a later message will get the same orderId.  It is my first time experimenting with such a designated client, so I am not really sure about the expected format of the messages, i.e. how can messages be grouped meaningfully if they all have the same orderId.

I am not sure what would be the best method to handle these, possibly:
- filter them out completely, so that no error message is reported
- put them in a predefined bucket for orderId 0
- something else?

issue_title: `to_image()` method does not take extra arguments
issue_body:
First of all, I'd just like to thank you so much for this incredible tool!

In the `README` it is stated that the `to_image()` method takes `conversion_kwargs` and links to [this](http://docs.wand-py.org/en/latest/wand/image.html#wand.image.Image) which has parameters such as `background`, yet the only parameter it actually seems to take is `resolution`. This leads to errors such as `to_image() got an unexpected keyword argument 'background'` when attempting to change the default background. Am I doing something wrong or does the documentation not quite match up with the functionality? If so is there a work-around or if not I'd be more than happy to contribute to try and add this functionality.

## README
| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
|`.to_image(**conversion_kwargs)`| Returns an instance of the `PageImage` class. For more details, see "[Visual debugging](#visual-debugging)" below. For conversion_kwargs, see [here](http://docs.wand-py.org/en/latest/wand/image.html#wand.image.Image).|

## Function
```python
    def to_image(self, resolution=None):
        """
        For conversion_kwargs, see http://docs.wand-py.org/en/latest/wand/image.html#wand.image.Image
        """
        from .display import PageImage, DEFAULT_RESOLUTION
        res = resolution or DEFAULT_RESOLUTION
        return PageImage(self, resolution=res)
```

issue_title: add custom json schema error message for improved readability on errors
Reshape layer determining dimensions of outputs
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is where I should be asking this question, so please let me know. I am going to use ShuffleNet to explain my question. 

In ShuffleNet, I have an input Node of dimension 5. This Node is transposed and outputs a node of shape [1,28,4,56,56]. This transposed Node, which we can define as OC2_DUMMY_2 is input into the next reshape layer. OC2_DUMMY_3, of int[4], is also input alongside the transposed Node, OC2_DUMMY_2. To my understanding, this means the Node of shape [1,28,4,56,56] is to be reshaped to a Node of 4 dimensions, gpu_0/shuffle_0_1. In the visualizer, this is what the above looks like:

```
Inputs:
    Data:   
        id: OC2_DUMMY_2
    Shape:
        id: OC2_DUMMY_3
        kind: Initializer
        type: int64[4]
        [
            1,
            112,
            56,
            56
        ]

Output:
    Reshaped:
        id: gpu_0/shuffle_0_1
```

How did the visualizer determine the output Node, when reshaped from a dimension of 5 to 4, will multiply the 2nd and 3rd dimensions (aka 28*4), to get the 112 for the 2nd dimension? Why didn't it do 4*56 or 56*56 to change the 3rd or 4th dimension? I know I am discussing one specific Reshape instance but I'm having the same confusion for all implementations of reshape in the visualizer. When I load the graph and get the .pb file, all the input DUMMY Nodes have for values is dim = 2 or dim = 4 in this case, and no mention of how the dimensions themselves will be resized. So where are the actual values of the dimensions themselves for the output Nodes being determined?

issue_title: Add a repeat feature for action
issue_body:
#### Description

The goal of this new feature is to be able to run an action repeatedly on threshold. For the moment an action is called only when the threshold is reached. For example, with the following configuration:

```
[load]
critical=5.0
critical_action=/home/myhome/bin/bipper.sh
```

The bipper.sh script will be called once when the load will be detected as critical.

The current issue proposes to implement a new configuration key (action_repeat):

```
[load]
critical=5.0
critical_action_repeat=/home/myhome/bin/bipper.sh
```

The bipper.sh will be ran at each refresh (by default every 3 seconds) as long as the critical load alert is active.

**Note: be aware that the script is ran every 3 seconds !!!**

issue_title: CPU Context Switch monitoring
issue_body:
PSUtil 4.1.0 introduces a new function to monitor the context switch (CS) (http://pythonhosted.org/psutil/#psutil.cpu_stats).

A context switch occurs when the kernel switches the processor from one thread to another—for example, when a thread with a higher priority than the running thread becomes ready. Context switching activity is important for several reasons. A program that monopolizes the processor lowers the rate of context switches because it does not allow much processor time for the other processes' threads. A high rate of context switching means that the processor is being shared repeatedly—for example, by many threads of equal priority. A high context-switch rate often indicates that there are too many threads competing for the processors on the system. (source https://technet.microsoft.com/en-us/library/cc938606.aspx).

The goal is to implement the monitoring of CPU CS into Glances:
- where ? CPU plugin ?
- optionnal ?
- limit rate ? (http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2794499/how-expensive-is-a-context-switch-is-it-better-to-implement-a-manual-task-switc)

Other resources:
- http://www.thomaskoetzing.de/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=196&Itemid=85

Here is a basic example of incorrect results for int data

    import pyodbc
    dbconn = pyodbc.connect("DSN=....")
    c = dbconn.cursor()
    c.execute("select 0, 1, 2")
    print c.fetchone()

    >> (210453397504, 210453397505, 210453397506)

All of the integers consistently add 2**32 * 2**7 (210453397504) to the correct value. This remains true until the value get to 2**31 (2147483648), at which point results are correctly converted to type Long. When I explicitly cast the data to numeric, it returns correctly:

    c.execute("select cast(0 as numeric), cast(1 as numeric), cast(2 as numeric)")
    print c.fetchone()

    >> (Decimal('0'), Decimal('1'), Decimal('2'))

Likewise if explicitly add a decimal to the values, it will return the correct values as Decimal instances

    c.execute("select 0.0, 1.0, 2.0")

    >> (Decimal('0'), Decimal('1'), Decimal('2'))

However when I try to cast the data as Ints, I get the same incorrect results as I do by default:

    c.execute("select cast(0 as int), cast(1 as int), cast(2 as int)")
    print c.fetchone()

    >> (210453397504, 210453397505, 210453397506)

There are a couple other users that have experienced the same issue on code.google.com
https://code.google.com/p/pyodbc/issues/detail?id=360

issue_title: varchar filtering slow with python strings
issue_body:
### Environment

- Python: 3.6
- pyodbc: 4.0.22
- OS: Ubuntu 16.04
- DB: MSSQL
- driver: ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server

### Issue

I have a database with varchar values I need to filter on based off of the value of a string in python, e.g.
```cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM my_database WHERE name=?", (string_name,))``` 

When I pass in a standard python ASCII string as a parameter, the query becomes extremely slow, with an execution time of 7 seconds. However, if I encode the string as 'utf-8', the execution is reduced to tens of milliseconds, e.g.
```cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM my_database WHERE name=?", (string_name.encode('utf-8'),))``` 

I understand that it is best practice to use 'utf-8' for this sort of thing, but would it be possible to add either some sort of warning or an attempt to auto-encode queries against varchar columns as 'utf-8'?

issue_title: allow for alternative delimiter
issue_body: we currently assume that the delimiter is of the type `{{` or `<<` but someone may want to do it like Elixir: `<%=` and we should ensure this doesn't trip the code up when we check if a line contains a variable by checking if it contains the first character of the delimiter.

issue_title: GPL3 licence means the project can't be used in many situations
issue_body:
This just a question as to whether Eppy is absolutely wedded to the GPL3 licence?

It's frustrating as this a project I'm really keen to use for more than just speeding up changes to IDF files. I'd like to be able to import it and use it in other Python software I develop. However the IP-sensitive nature of the clients (universities, government, management consultancies) means that any import which is released under GPL3 licence is off-limits due to the "copyleft" aspect - the part which means that any work which imports Eppy must also be released under GPL3.

Is this intentional? Or could the licence be changed to something (such as the MIT licence) which would be more palatable to end-users with IP concerns?

issue_title: Python3 transition - alternate strategy
issue_body:
Right now we are using 2to3 to transition from python2 to python3. So we have 
- python2 code that is unit tested
- 2to3 is used to generate python3 code
- python3 code that is unit tested
- setup.py has to jiggled to allow for seamless installation of python2 or python3 as appropriate

Another option is too use the package [six](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/six)
From six's documentation:
*"Six provides simple utilities for wrapping over differences between Python 2 and Python 3. It is intended to support codebases that work on both Python 2 and 3 without modification".*

Essentially this allows to write and maintain only one code base, that will run in python2 and python3. @digwanderlust thinks this is a viable and preferred option. I am inclined to trust his judgement on this

The advantage is using six is that:
- we write maintain and test only one code base.
- pip install is straight forward

@eayoungs , If we go down the six route, issue #64 becomes redundant. Sorry to muddy the situation like this.

I suggest, we proceed in the following manner:
- One or more of us try out six with eppy - as a proof concept
- Work continues on issue #64, unless it becomes very clear that six is the way to go

issue_title: Wrong transitions in binaries
issue_body:
pytzdata version >= `2017.1` has wrong timezone transitions data in compiled binary files. 
I found this with this pendulum usage, but since pendulum only uses pytzdata tz database, I report the issue here. The following code wrongly raises `pendulum.tz.exceptions.AmbiguousTime: The datetime 2018-06-20 19:50:00 is ambiguous.`.
```
import pendulum


pendulum.set_transition_rule(pendulum.TRANSITION_ERROR)
print(pendulum.from_timestamp(1529524200).replace(tzinfo='Asia/Dubai'))
```

The point is, that **Asia/Dubai** currently does not observe DST, but somehow, following 
`<Transition [2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC, 2038-01-19 07:14:07 -> 2038-01-19 07:14:07]>`
transition is defined for the **Asia/Dubai** timezone according to timezone binary file. Observe that transition is weird in a way that no shift will be made at all (pre and post transition times are the same).

Such weird transitions (year=2038 with pre and post transition times being the same) occur also in **America/Lima**, **America/Guayaquil**, **America/Bogota** and maybe in many others.

Note that in pytzdata version `2016.10` (and pendulum `0.7.0`), everything seems to be ok (i.e., the code above does not raise error and computes correct date time), since there are no such weird 2038-year's transitions.

May it be the case, that binaries with timezones are wrongly compiled from the Olson tz database? 

Thanx, for handling this issue

Jozef

Read some description from the `conky` manual page.
http://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/artful/man1/conky.1.html

```
NAME

       conky - A system monitor for X originally based on the torsmo code, but
       more kickass. It just keeps on given'er. Yeah.

DESCRIPTION

       Conky is a system monitor for X originally based on torsmo.  Since  its
       inception,  Conky has changed significantly from its predecessor, while
       maintaining simplicity and  configurability.  Conky  can  display  just
       about  anything,  either on your root desktop or in its own window. Not
       only does Conky have many built-in objects, it can  also  display  just
       about  any  piece  of  information  by using scripts and other external
       programs.

       Conky has more than 250 built  in  objects,  including  support  for  a
       plethora  of OS stats (uname, uptime, CPU usage, mem usage, disk usage,
       "top" like process stats, and network monitoring, just to name a  few),
       built in IMAP and POP3 support, built in support for many popular music

This has more than 250 built-in objects. I think it'd be nice to have a conky wrapper module similar to `i3blocks` and `i3py3status`... and maybe, to split off `i3status` wrapper too. This may be right up someone's alley.

issue_title: Debugging to a rotating file handler
issue_body:
The current `--debug` command line option takes a list of named debugging access points and attaches a `StreamHandler` which sends the output to `sys.stderr`.  I would like an additional way to send the debugging output to a `RotatingFileHandler` by providing a file name, and optionally `maxBytes` and `backupCount`.  For example, this invocation sends the main application debugging to standard error and the debugging output of the `bacpypes.udp` module to the `traffic.txt` file:

```
$ python some_application --debug __main__ bacpypes.udp:traffic.txt
```

By default the `maxBytes` is zero so there is no rotating file, but it can be provided, for example this limits the file size to 1MB:

```
$ python some_application --debug __main__ bacpypes.udp:traffic.txt:1048576
```

If `maxBytes` is provided, then by default the `backupCount` is 10, but it can also be specified, so this limits the output to one hundred files:

```
$ python some_application --debug __main__ bacpypes.udp:traffic.txt:1048576:100
```

So the definition of DEBUG becomes:

```
positional arguments:
    --debug [DEBUG [ DEBUG ... ]]
        DEBUG ::= debugger [ : fileName [ : maxBytes [ : backupCount ]]]
```

The justification is that I quite often run the same application many times with multiple debugging options, first adding more debugging to understand more about what an application is doing and then removing them to temper the volume of debugging output.  By redirecting debugging output to a file, I can limit the amount of output to the screen and dig into the details by looking at the output files when needed.  I also would like the option for other BACpypes to run their applications and send me just one particular layer of debugging for their application without needing them to send everything.

issue_title: Model function itself instead of name of it
issue_body:
It would be more convenient if the function itself is passed such as 

`fitModels(GradientBoostingRegressor(), gbm_paramGrid, X_train, y_train)`

instead of the current syntax which requires the name of the model function, i.e.,
`fitModels(GradientBoostingRegressor, gbm_paramGrid, X_train, y_train)`

issue_title: spec2nexus does not read metadata properly for USAXS

issue_title: Mulitple arguments per switch
issue_body:
It would be nice if there were a way to have switches that requires multiple arguments.
For one of my program I wrote in `bash`, I deal with hostnames like this:
```sh
myapp --host www.example.com [other params]
```
But I can also write this:
```sh
myapp --host example.com www [other params]
```
The second one deals better with the autocompletion as you specify the domain before the subdomain, and thus, completion is able to propose you all subdomains of the domain.

The problem is: I haven't found a way to do this with plumbum.
It would be nice to allow such kind of parsing.

Here is an example of how I would like to write such Application:
```python
from plumbum import cli

# Very basic check for a FQDN
def is_fqdn(hostname):
  return hostname.count(".") >= 2

# The first element of the tuple is the number of parameters eaten/used
# The second element of the tuple is the actual object parsed
# Maybe a class would be better...
@cli.SwitchParser
def fqdn_parser(*args):
  if len(args) == 0:
    raise TypeError("Not a FQDN")
  fqdn = args[0]
  if is_fqdn(fqdn):
    return (1, fqdn)
  if len(args) < 2:
    raise TypeError("Not a FQDN")
  fqdn = "{}.{}".format(args[1], args[0])
  if is_fqdn(fqdn):
    return (2, fqdn)
  raise TypeError("Not a FQDN")

class MyApp(cli.Application):
  hostname = cli.SwitchAttr(["-h, "--host"], str, parser = fqdn_parser, mandatory = True)

  def main(self):
    print("Hostname: {}".format(self.hostname))

if __name__ == "__main__":
  MyApp.run()
```
Output:
```sh
$ myapp -h example.com www
Hostname: www.example.com
$ myapp -h www.example.com
Hostname: www.example.com
```

Of course, for my case, the biggset problem is the autocompletion. So when the autocompletion will be available, it would also be nice if we could write our own completors. If the parser is a class, we could just add a `.complete()` method to implement custom completors.

It would also be nice if we could have such parsing features also for positional parameters, ie: forbid the presence of switch between parameters when they have to be parsed together.
```sh
myapp example.com -v www # This should be forbidden
```

I hope this feature could be added soon to plumbum, and thank you for you work.

issue_title: Serving WSGI app from Python code
issue_body:
`aiohttp-wsgi` has a way to serve WSGI apps from the CLI, but there is no easy way to do it from Python code. By easy I mean with a single function call.

The `serve` function in `__main__` is almost good for this use case, except that it expects args from the CLI.

For inspiration, I really like how waitress does it with [`waitress.serve`](https://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/waitress/en/latest/#usage).

issue_title: Behavior of read() and read_one() when input device disappears
issue_body:
Hi there. I'm trying to implement a little script which watches for a certain bluetooth button device to appear, then read events from it, and handle gracefully if it disappears.

I actually found your post on https://stackoverflow.com/questions/15944987/python-evdev-detect-device-unplugged/16500472#16500472 and I'm using that as an example.

However, the interesting thing is that when my bluetooth device goes to sleep, it appears that select() returns with descriptor in the read array. When I go and call read() or read_one() on the corresponding InputDevice I get:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "key_controller.py", line 67, in <module>
    event = dev.read_one()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/evdev/eventio.py", line 54, in read_one
    event = _input.device_read(self.fd)
IOError: [Errno 19] No such device
```

So interestingly, it seems that it takes a while for udev to actually tell me that the device is gone (so that I can tear down the fd in the selected set. I dealt with this by just doing an os.path.exists() on the devices' .fn field, which seems to work.

Not sure if this should be considered a bug or expected behavior, but given these devices can appear and disappear, it might be nice for the python evdev api to be able to handle that more gracefully somehow. I expected read_one() to return None in this case, and for there to maybe be some kind of other flag I could check to see if this InputDevice instance was still "valid".

issue_title: Declaration -> Schema
issue_body:
Config declaration is essentially a schema so that's what it should be called.

Maybe it makes sense to separate Config initialisation from Schema declaration?

```
config_schema = Schema(...)
config = config_schema.get_config()
```

In Schema, one would also specify where the configuration is being loaded from and where it would be written to if user decided to save it.

issue_title: ConfigParserAdapter.write and JsonAdapter.write consistency
issue_body:
* [ ] `.write()` should by default have `with_defaults=True` so that it is consistent with other adapters -- this way all exporting commands will by default include defaults.
* [ ] `.write(with_defaults=True)` should behave similarly to `Config.to_dict` which quietly excludes items that have no value set.

Update. I am not sure this should have `with_defaults=True`!
Maybe JsonAdapter should change its behaviour. Because writing defaults is different to writing customisation and definitely less frequent.
It's ok that to_dict defaults with_defaults=True

issue_title: Watch with no etcd up - Infinite loop
issue_body:
Hi, 

When we try, to add a watch but etcd is not running, there is no exception raise or status failed return.

In the next example, I try to connect the with the wrong port. No issue for client creation, but when I create my watch, etcd3 never return.

```
>>> import etcd3
>>> c=etcd3.client(host='127.0.0.1', port='2378', timeout=3)
>>> c.watch_once('1111')
```

In the watch.py file (add_callback method), the watch queue is not receiving the watcher timeout.

I think at this line:
`return self._watch_id_queue.get()`
should send the timeout value:
`return self._watch_id_queue.get(timeout=self.timeout)`

In this case, the add_callback can catch the Empty exception sent by queue.Queue.

Best regards,

issue_title: Handle 404 (cursor not found) during batch processing
issue_body:
Once the client goes into batch processing mode, it always looks for the "result" attribute in the HTTP response without checking if it's a valid HTTP response in the first place. issue_title: Proxies lose proxy-ness inside wrapped methods
issue_body:
I'm using `ObjectProxy` to create an object `x2` that acts just like another object `x`, except that I intercept one method `inner()`:

```
import wrapt

class C(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def outer(self):
        return self.inner()

    def inner(self):
        return 7

class CProxy(wrapt.ObjectProxy):

    def __init__(self, wrapped):
        super().__init__(wrapped)

    def inner(self):
        return 9
```

I could use a normal subclass for this, but 1) I want identity to be preserved so that e.g. `x = [original]; proxy in x` returns `True`, 2) I want any modifications of `proxy` to propagate back to `original`, and 3) I don't create the `original` object (I'm only passed it as an instance), nor do I have control of its lifecycle, I just proxy it & pass it to a method that will eventually call `inner()` or `outer()` or whatever methods it wants.  So this is a great use case for a proxy.

However, as the following shows, the `C.outer()` method gets a `self` that's no longer the proxy object, so when it calls `self.inner()`, I lose the desired highjacking:

```
original = C()
proxy = CProxy(original)

print(proxy.inner())
# 9
print(proxy.outer())
# 7
```

Is this expected behavior?  If so, is there some other way for me to achieve this "pinpoint overriding of methods for an instance"?

I'm using version 1.10.10 of `wrapt` on Python 3.6.1.  Thanks.

issue_title: Multiple Intent entries in @ask.intent()
msgpack._packer issue
issue_body:
This is similar to issue #6.

`_packer` is an internal variable of msgpack and if cython is not available when installing msgpack, then it would not be available as well. In this case msgpack will fallback to pure python installation and will not compile its cython extensions.

Does msgpack_numpy explicitly requires those cython extensions to function properly? If yes, then it is a good idea to mention it in documents to inform users about it.

issue_title: Question concerning stable order of sequence elements when converted to json lists
Invalid datetime decoding
issue_body:
Do not use tzinfo in datetime/time constuctor with pytz. This will produce datetime/time objects with incorrect timezone in most cases (from pytz documentation):

> Unfortunately using the tzinfo argument of the standard datetime constructors ”does not work” with pytz for many timezones.
> 
> \>>> datetime(2002, 10, 27, 12, 0, 0, tzinfo=amsterdam).strftime(fmt)
> '2002-10-27 12:00:00 LMT+0020'
> It is safe for timezones without daylight saving transitions though, such as UTC:
> 
> \>>> datetime(2002, 10, 27, 12, 0, 0, tzinfo=pytz.utc).strftime(fmt)
> '2002-10-27 12:00:00 UTC+0000'

so, the proper way of constructing datetime/time objects with timezone is to create naive object, then use localize method of pytz timezone class. like this:

`>>> amsterdam.localize(datetime(2002, 10, 27, 12, 0, 0)).strftime(fmt)`
`'2002-10-27 12:00:00 CET+0100'`

issue_title: Cant decode token (invalid start byte)
issue_body:
Hi, 
i try to discover the vacuum robot on macos.
`mirobo discover` does find it, but cannot decode or show the token:

> (dustcloud-PBjoGnIX) bash-3.2$ mirobo discover --handshake true
> INFO:miio.device:Sending discovery to <broadcast> with timeout of 5s..
> WARNING:miio.device:error while reading discover results: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xb2 in position 3: invalid start byte
> (dustcloud-PBjoGnIX) bash-3.2$ 5~

Is there some kind of workaround?
Or is it possible to write the token undecoded to a file?

issue_title: 'Line' Not Allowed in Histogram
issue_body:

issue_title: geocoder.base.Base should not call sys.exit()
issue_body:
The decision on how to handle a KeyboardInterrupt exception should be left up to the application, not a module.  [geocoder.base.Base._connect()](https://github.com/DenisCarriere/geocoder/blob/master/geocoder/base.py) currently does this:
```python
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            sys.exit()
```
Which takes the decision on how to handle a KeyboardInterrupt away from the application making use of `geocoder.*`.

issue_title: Interval removal breaks this tree
issue_body:
I ran `chop` on a few thousand `IntervalTree`s, and found that it crashed on several of them. Looking at the library code showed that `remove` failed to find `Interval`s where they were supposed to be. I tested until I found something more consistent.

Here, I add 9 intervals to an empty tree (in sorted order, so I could have used an iterable). Then, I remove the interval in the root node, leaving the tree with a broken node. Trying to remove one of the intervals left under that node then raises a `KeyError`.

```
>>> from intervaltree import IntervalTree
>>> t = IntervalTree()
>>> t.addi(860, 917, 1)
>>> t.addi(860, 917, 2)
>>> t.addi(860, 917, 3)
>>> t.addi(860, 917, 4)
>>> t.addi(871, 917, 1)
>>> t.addi(871, 917, 2)
>>> t.addi(871, 917, 3)
>>> t.addi(961, 986, 1)
>>> t.addi(1047, 1064, 1)
>>> t.addi(1047, 1064, 2)
>>> t.removei(961, 986, 1) # remove root node. t.verify() would fail now
>>> t.removei(871, 917, 3)
Node<871, depth=2, balance=0>
 Interval(860, 917, 1)
 Interval(860, 917, 2)
 Interval(860, 917, 3)
 Interval(860, 917, 4)
<:  Node<871, depth=1, balance=0>
     Interval(871, 917, 1)
     Interval(871, 917, 2)
     Interval(871, 917, 3)
>:  Node<1047, depth=1, balance=0>
     Interval(1047, 1064, 1)
     Interval(1047, 1064, 2)

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/intervaltree/intervaltree.py", line 372, in removei
    return self.remove(Interval(begin, end, data))
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/intervaltree/intervaltree.py", line 361, in remove
    self.top_node = self.top_node.remove(interval)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/intervaltree/node.py", line 211, in remove
    return self.remove_interval_helper(interval, done, should_raise_error=True)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/intervaltree/node.py", line 248, in remove_interval_helper
    raise KeyError(interval)
KeyError: Interval(871, 917, 3)
```

That's with Python 2.7; the same behavior exists in Python 3.4.

issue_title: plyfile is missing version info
issue_body:
The plyfile module is missing the `__version__` field. This can be added by including a line 
```
__version__ = '0.4'
```
in the file `plyfile.py`. If the version needs to be synchronized with the `setup.py` the best thing is to parse the version number in `setup.py` from the source code.

````diff
diff --git a/internetarchive/cli/ia_upload.py b/internetarchive/cli/ia_upload.py
index 6aaff15..5160820 100644
--- a/internetarchive/cli/ia_upload.py
+++ b/internetarchive/cli/ia_upload.py
@@ -229,7 +229,7 @@ def main(argv, session):
                 item = session.get_item(identifier)
                 # TODO: Clean up how indexed metadata items are coerced
                 # into metadata.
-                md_args = ['{0}:{1}'.format(k.lower(), v) for (k, v) in row.items() if v]
+                md_args = ['{0}:{1}'.format(k.decode("utf-8").lower(), v.decode("utf-8")) for (k, v) in row.items() if v]
                 metadata = get_args_dict(md_args)
                 upload_kwargs['metadata'].update(metadata)
                 r = _upload_files(item, local_file, upload_kwargs, prev_identifier,

issue_title: Add support for p2 instances
issue_body:
We recently started a project using a p2.xlarge instance, as a result we get:

  ERROR:awslimitchecker.services.ec2:ERROR - unknown instance type 'p2.xlarge'; not counting

There are also p2.8xlarge and p2.16xlarge instances available, according to https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/instance-types/p2/ and https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/instance-types/#p2. Apparently these are intended for general GPU use for parallel computation, where the g2 types are more specifically for graphics-intensive uses.

ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'Unlimited'
```

Sure enough, as of this morning, the Trusted Advisor check for EC2 service, "On-Demand instances - t2.micro" in us-west-2 has started returning a value of "Unlimited" instead of something that can be parsed as an integer.

I'll need to:

1. modify the TrustedAdvisor code to handle this. And ideally modify the TA code so anything else that's a non-integer (since apparently we can't count on "Limit Amount" actually being a numeric amount, because AWS's API specifications are really just taken as suggestions) just triggers an error-level log message.
2. Figure out how to represent "unlimited" internally. Do we set something to e.g. MAXINT, use a magic value like -1, just stop looking at the limit, or - probably most likely - set an attribute on the AWSLimit object that just stops it from being alerted on.

issue_title: Request: optionally allow remaining bytes in stream
issue_body:
The JavaObjectUnmarshaller.readObject method currently emits a warning when there are remaining bytes present beyond the serialized Java object. It would be useful to have a way to suppress that warning in cases where further data in the input stream is expected and normal.

Motivating use case is unserializing SecretKey objects from JCEKS keystores, as done in the pyjks project (https://github.com/doublereedkurt/pyjks). The JCEKS keystore format is almost entirely custom structured binary data, with the exception of SecretKey entries which are stored as wholesale serialized Java objects embedded right in the middle of the file.

Keystore entries can appear in any order and mix of types, and there are always raw signature bytes present at the end of a keystore, so it is fully expected for there to be trailing data after a call to readObject. There is no way of knowing in advance what the size of a serialized Java object is either, so we also can't get around it by feeding a subset of the stream to javaobj.

So my request is: could we have a flag to indicate that trailing data is expected, in which case no warning is emitted?

issue_title: Allow for URL settings to be callables
issue_body:
I'd like to propose that the settings for defining custom URLs (e. g. SOCIAL_AUTH_NEW_USER_REDIRECT_URL, SOCIAL_AUTH_NEW_ASSOCIATION_REDIRECT_URL, etc.) can be defined as callables.

For example an implementation might want to add a parameter to the URL: 

```python
SOCIAL_AUTH_NEW_ASSOCIATION_REDIRECT_URL = lambda x: reverse('user_settings_account') + '?next=/'
```

The reasoning behind that is that even though I could use ``reverse_lazy``, it is a proxy object and thus cannot be concatenated with a string. If the setting is a callable, it is called when the URL patterns are loaded, so that ``reverse`` can be used.

Would you approve of that? I could implement a patch/ pull request in that case.

issue_title: The orphan signal list bug
issue_body:
Situation:
1. Process A acquires lock.
2. Process B blocks.
3. Process B unblocks cause of expire (or failure).
4. Process A releases lock and pushes to signal list.

Now system is a broken state, cause if 2 processes come again:
1. Process A acquires lock.
2. Process B fails to `set nx`, but doesn't block cause of orphan element in a signal list.

This could be mitigated if `set nx` command is wrapped into lua script, which deletes signal list. Rule: signal list should be empty upon acquire.

issue_title: Variable types in the documentation
issue_body:
In this page of the documentation: 
[StaticDataApiV3](http://riot-watcher.readthedocs.io/en/latest/riotwatcher/StaticDataApiV3.html#riotwatcher._apis.StaticDataApiV3)

In the function `champions(region, locale=None, version=None, tags=None, data_by_id=None)`, the parameter `data_by_id` is documented as having the type `bool`. I tested and by using `data_by_id=True` I got a Bad Request error. Changed it to `data_by_id="true"` and it worked. This means that the wrapper is not converting from True to "true" and instead it's using the value directly into the URL. So in the URL it's becoming `dataById=True`.

Is RiotWatcher using the values directly in the URL instead of converting from Python types to JSON types?

issue_title: Inconsistent error handling
issue_body:
I got an HTTPError from a list.create (BTW why is this not lists.create?) with no explanation at all. Looking at the source, I can see that _post raises HTTPError(request=r) with r being the response and not the request (I suppose that's a bug). _get, _delete, and _patch/_put all do different things. This makes it really hard to code robustly against.

I don't know if some of this is due to MailChimp having an inconsistent API, but here are some suggestions:

- All methods examine the returned result consistently and raise an exception upon getting back a not-OK status

- The exception could inherit from requests.exceptions.RequestException with request and response being filled in, and message being the detail field from the JSON that MailChimp returns, if available

That way, as a user of the library it would be easy to get started and debug what's wrong, and also easy to add error handling later (just slap a try: ... except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e: around the call).

issue_title: Better separate handling of access tokens and refresh tokens
issue_body:
Right now access tokens and refresh tokens are JWT. The library requires the claim 'type' to tell them apart, this makes the library not friendly to external token providers.

From what I've seen, there is no need for refresh tokens to be JWT, they could be base64-encoded random strings, as seen on auth0's blog: https://auth0.com/blog/refresh-tokens-what-are-they-and-when-to-use-them/ This solution would mean that the code securing endpoint with jwt would not need to worry about finding some custom claims in the tokens, meaning it could also accept external token providers.

Of course, this would most probably mean breaking backwards compatibility, (especially access tokens and blacklists)

I wanted to open the ticket to gather thoughts and opinions on this. It's something I'd like to get integrated into the library as it benefits me and I think it could benefit the library as well, it's something I could implement. (at least after returning from vacations)

issue_title: Optimize DocxTemplate for rerendering the same template multiple times
issue_body:
I have a single template that I render over and over again with different parameters in my program. It takes about half a second per render. I'm wondering if it'd be possible to do some of the parsing and transformation once and then render multiple times with that parsed state to lower the overall runtime.

A representative call to render for my template takes .53 seconds. Breaking down the components of that:

* .24 seconds in build_xml
    * .16 seconds in get_xml and patch_xml
    * .08 seconds in render_xml
* .26 seconds in map_tree

Of that, the .16 seconds in get and patch xml seem like they could be done once. I'm also wondering if the .26 seconds in map_tree could be made faster in lxml if we could guarantee the template were already in the namespace of the source document or something along those lines.

If I *could* do those pieces once, that would be ~80% of the render time, which would definitely be worthwhile for me. Assuming it didn't change the external API, would a patch to do that be interesting?

issue_title: handle utf-8 decoding errors in fortls
issue_body:
In langserver.py have extended the try catch block in def init_file(file path) to also include the two with statements. This handles utf-8 decoding errors in the PY3K relevant code:

`def init_file(filepath):
    #
    try:
        if PY3K:

            with open(filepath, 'r', encoding="utf-8") as fhandle:
                contents = re.sub(r'\t', r'  ', fhandle.read())
                contents_split = contents.splitlines()

        else:
            with open(filepath, 'r') as fhandle:
                contents = re.sub(r'\t', r'  ', fhandle.read())
                contents_split = contents.splitlines()
    #

        fixed_flag = detect_fixed_format(contents_split)
        ast_new = process_file(contents_split, True, filepath, fixed_flag)
    except:
        return None
    else:
        # Construct new file object and add to workspace
        tmp_obj = {
            "contents": contents_split,
            "ast": ast_new,
            "fixed": fixed_flag
        }
        return tmp_obj`

issue_title: Using Regex Flags with DRS
issue_body:
Hey all,

We've had to make our API fully case insensitive, and we've implemented this using a regex flag, like this:
```
urlpatterns = patterns('',
    url(r'^(?i)widget/', include(apps.widget)),
    url(r'^(?i)widget2/', include('apps.widget2')),
    url(r'^(?i)docs/', include('rest_framework_swagger.urls')),
)
```
Unfortunately this breaks our swagger documentation. The /docs/ endpoint then tries to make a call like `/docs/api-docs/{var}widget`, which doesn't return anything, and so we end up with no documented endpoints. It seems like the issue is caused by some sort of conversion of flags to {var} which doesn't play nice with Django urlpatterns. Is there any way around this? Perhaps using middleware to adjust the url?

issue_title: OrderedDict missing
issue_body:
Hi. I tried to use your serializer with the latest django rest framework. 

So I get such an error:
```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/rest_framework_mongoengine/utils.py", line 8, in <module>
    from rest_framework.compat import OrderedDict
ImportError: cannot import name 'OrderedDict'
```

It seems that  `OrderedDict`  was removed from `from rest_framework.compat` because of python3. So I guess you need to add a `try ... catch` statement and try to import it from `collections` in case of python 3.


There is even a PR but it simply tries to take `OrderedDict` from the `collections`. Maybe that's not what you want.

issue_title: Have deleted an order through API but am able to find it still through API ? Is there a reason?
issue_body:
Am implementing a private app using the API and I've generated a couple orders. Am now implementing the functionality to delete these "api generated" orders if required.

I'm using:

    try:
       order = shopify.Order.find(int(order_number))
       #order.cancel()
       print("Attempting to delete order #: " + order_number)
       order.destroy()
    except:
       ....

This appears to work and the order is getting deleted in the front-end Shopify dashboard. However, through the API I am **still** able to retrieve the order data for the order I apparently just deleted?! Why is this? 

E.g. I can still call:  *order = shopify.Order.find(int(order_number))*, where *order_number* is the id I just deleted. It will retrieve an order object with all the data that I apparently just deleted. Some kind of soft deletion caching going on? Will I no longer be able to retrieve this after a day or two?

Is *destory()* the correct method to be calling to delete an order in the system? Or should we be calling something such as *order.delete()*, where we may actually receive a connection response which we can then check?

Also, I can still see the order data when navigating my web browser to:
*https://API_KEY:API_PASS@mystore.myshopify.com/admin/orders/order_number.json*

Shouldn't it be deleted and gone?

Is there a way through the API to verify if the order object has already been deleted (through API) ?

This issue is similar to the following post which never seemed to get a response on whether it in fact was an issue/bug or a feature ? 
https://ecommerce.shopify.com/c/shopify-apis-and-technology/t/shopify-api-how-does-order-deletion-work-221931

P.S do we still need to cancel an order before deleting it (like you do in the admin dashboard?) or through the API can we directly delete an order the cancellation is automated?

Thanks!

Paul

issue_title: ndarray doesn't support  Ellipsis operation
issue_body: If we want to slice high dimension array, for example array A with shape (4,5,6,7), we need to explicitly slice each dimension, A[:, :, :, idx], rather than A[..., idx] in numpy, which is concision and easy to debug.

## Test Code 
```
import numpy as np
import mxnet as mx


def test_dilate_cpu():
    input_data = mx.symbol.Variable(name="data")
    ctx = mx.cpu(0)

    e = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=input_data, kernel =(3,1),pad=(2,0),dilate =(2,1), num_filter = 1,num_group=1,name="uo",no_bias=True)
    e = mx.sym.MakeLoss(e)

    data = np.arange(0,60)
    data = data.reshape([1, 1, 30, 2])
    # print data
    data = mx.nd.array(data,ctx=ctx)
    weight_value = np.array([1, 1, 1])
    weight_value = weight_value.reshape([1, 1,3, 1])
    weight_value = mx.nd.array(weight_value,ctx=ctx)
    out_grad = mx.nd.array(np.zeros(data.shape),ctx=ctx)
    exe1 = e.bind(ctx, {"data": data,'uo_weight':weight_value},args_grad=[out_grad,weight_value])

    weight_value = mx.nd.array(weight_value,ctx=ctx)
    out_grad = mx.nd.array(np.zeros(data.shape),ctx=ctx)
    exe1 = e.bind(ctx, {"data": data,'uo_weight':weight_value},args_grad=[out_grad,weight_value])

    y = exe1.forward(is_train=True)
    exe1.backward()
    print "code in gpu", y[0].asnumpy()


test_dilate_cpu()
test_dilate_gpu()

issue_title: Don't wheel packages installed from VCS urls
issue_body:
If a user specifies a git package URL to install, it will get wheeled with whatever version is in `setup.py` for the package.

However, there are many cases where this is undesired behavior:

* Installing a commit on top of `master` which is ahead of the latest release.
* Testing a feature branch where the version hasn't been changed to something unique.
* Testing a feature branch where you use a unique version, but forget to change the version when adding more commits on your feature branch.

I think most of these cases would be unexpected for the regular developer.

This has the potential to pollute your cache with versions that don't match the actual version. Especially on CI, this can cause problems.

Here's a currently-failing test: https://i.fluffy.cc/Xr3wxKM7cPb5tvMLnGBc9H9F2QV5ptjn.html

issue_title: Failed to create state directory on startup on Windows
issue_body:
At https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1385380, a user is seeing an error message running `watchman.exe version`: `failed to create /tmp\kats-state: Not a directory`.

Apparently the user doesn't have any environment variables related to temporary directories set. Yes, this is a bit wonky and their environment is likely buggy. However, main.cpp is currently falling back to `/tmp` on Windows (`watchman_tmp_dir = get_env_with_fallback("TMPDIR", "TMP", "/tmp");`). The use of `/tmp` as a default path name on Windows is not ideal and is likely causing this failure. IIRC `/tmp` will likely get translated to `c:\tmp`, which most likely doesn't exist. We'll likely want the default to be `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Temp` (at least for modern versions of Windows).

issue_title: Run SetupMultilingualSite after installing PAM
issue_body:
Thats my scenario:
I have a package holding GenericSetup configs like registry.xml including language-settings (which languages are used, default language and negotiation for example). Also i want to install dependencies. PAM and others in this case.

So installing my package does the settings correct and also installs PAM. So far so good.

But the LRF and LIF folders are not created. Therefore i had to go to Plone's control panel -> language settings and hit "save" without any changes done to trigger the creation process of PAM.

I found out that i can trigger that after my package installation by using plone.app.multilingual.browser.setup.py's code
```
setupTool = SetupMultilingualSite()
setupTool.setupSite(<portal>)
```
inside my setuphandler (using GS 1.8.2+ i can use post_handler on my profile's registry)

I don't think this should be in my package as this seems pretty default to me, to get the language folders and all other stuff set, when having my configs and installing PAM, when building a new portal.

My first thought was: why doesn't PAM run this on installation as it doesn't override/replace any existing content or delete stuff during that process.

**plone.app.multilingual**
configure.zcml
```
  <genericsetup:registerProfile
      name="default"
      title="plone.app.multilingual"
      description="Install to enable multilingual content support"
      directory="profiles/default"
      provides="Products.GenericSetup.interfaces.EXTENSION"
      for="Products.CMFPlone.interfaces.IPloneSiteRoot"
      post_handler=".setuphandlers.post_install_default"/>
```
setuphanders.py
```
def post_install_default(tool):
    from plone.app.multilingual.browser.setup import SetupMultilingualSite
    from plone.api import portal
    setupTool = SetupMultilingualSite()
    setupTool.setupSite(portal.get())
```
The other idea by @pbauer: add a second profile depending on the default profile that i can trigger as a dependency in my package then.
configure.zcml
```
  <genericsetup:registerProfile
      name="setup"
      title="plone.app.multilingual (Setup)"
      description="Install and enable multilingual content support"
      directory="profiles/setup"
      provides="Products.GenericSetup.interfaces.EXTENSION"
      for="Products.CMFPlone.interfaces.IPloneSiteRoot"
      post_handler=".setuphandlers.post_setup"/>
```
metadata.xml
```
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<metadata>
  <version>0.1</version>
  <dependencies>
    <dependency>profile-plone.app.multilingual:default</dependency>
  </dependencies>
</metadata>
```
setuphanders.py
```
def setup(tool):
    from plone.app.multilingual.browser.setup import SetupMultilingualSite
    from plone.api import portal
    setupTool = SetupMultilingualSite()
    setupTool.setupSite(portal.get())
```

**my metadata.xml** would look like that then:
```
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<metadata>
  <version>0.1</version>
  <dependencies>
    <dependency>profile-plone.app.multilingual:setup</dependency>
  </dependencies>
</metadata>
```

Any considerations?
Better ideas?

issue_title: Performance Improvements for Asyncio
issue_body:
To decide whether I should migrate my codes from [aiozmq](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiozmq) to the vanilla pyzmq, I have created a benchmark based on the examples here: https://github.com/achimnol/asyncio-zmq-benchmark , using a single-process push/pull (producer/consumer) pattern via a local TCP socket.

The result shows that aiozmq performs better than pyzmq.
Both the pyzmq and aiozmq internally uses poller based on `zmq_poll` to implement custom asyncio event loops, but the performance of aiozmq is a way better and aiozmq allows the event loop replaced with different implementations such as [uvloop](https://github.com/MagicStack/uvloop).
I suspect that PyZMQ's frequent context switching due to an extra poller and send coroutines may be causing severe performance penalties, though we need more detailed profiling results.

Would be there some ways to improve this?

issue_title: change readthedocs and versioning
issue_body:
Issue #146 came about because RTD is set up to build master tip. So any time we merge changes, it updates the docs. Thus the docs can get out of sync with the released version.

This is compounded by two other issues:

1. after we do a release, we don't then update the version to 0.x.y.dev0 or something like that (I do that with my projects) -- if we did this, then the docs would show that they apply to a different version than the released one
2. after we do a release, we don't then update RTD to build docs for that tag as well (I do this with my projects sometimes) -- if we did this, then users would see that the docs they're looking at apply to a different version and can then switch to the version they want to see

The place in RTD to update versions is here:

https://readthedocs.org/projects/mozilla-django-oidc/

But only people who have access to that can do it.

This issue can be fixed entirely by adjusting the release process. I don't know if there's a good way to automate either of them because I do them by hand, but that's probably worth looking into.

I think this is worth fixing process-wise.

How do we want to deal with this?

issue_title: Nested configurables
issue_body:
We're looking to use traitlets to enforce type checking in the configuration of a coastal ocean model thetis (github.com/thetisproject/thetis).

Right now, we have a tree of configuration options.  For example:
```
ModelOptions:
   turbulence_model = "some_value"
   turbulence_model_options = TurbulenceModelOptions
```

Right now, we just use nested dictionaries for this.

It seems like one would like to have a `Configurable` with traits that are themselves `Configurable` objects:

right now we use:

```python
class TurbulenceModelOptions(Configurable):
    pass

class GLSOptions(TurbulenceModelOptions):
   use_hyperviscosity = Bool()

class SmagorinskyOptions(TurbulenceModelOptions):
    background_viscosity = Float()

class ModelOptions(Configurable):
    turbulence_model = Enum(["gls", "smagorinsky"])
    turbulence_model_options = Instance(TurbulenceModelOptions)
```

But generating (for example) rST doesn't recurse into the trait values, so the presentation of the options isn't right.

Are we doing this wrong?  Is there some more obvious way of glueing together such a hierarchy?

issue_title: Need way to override client config when creating resource (for s3v4 sigs)
issue_body:
I would like a way to programatically force a resource to use s3v4 signatures when the endpoint config doesn't specify that the endpoint is using v4 signatures

When creating a resource as follows:
```
import boto3
resource = boto3.resource('s3')
```
... the `boto3.session.resource()` method hard codes a botocore.Client config at https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/1.0.1/boto3/session.py#L264 to be passed into the client that's internally created for the resource.

The only way I can see to force v4 signatures is to set it inside the .aws/config file via the very under-documented `aws configure set default.s3.signature_version s3v4` that I found in https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/377 .

I need v4 signatures in the context of creating presigned URLs in combination with server side key management via KMS. I try to use `resource.meta.client.generate_presigned_url()`. Yes I could create a separate client and force my own config within that client (since `boto3.client()` does allow a config argument, but that 1) creates inconsistency for v4 access for presigned URLs, v1 access otherwise, 2) requires me to have separate objects, thus repeating myself, and 3) still doesn't allow other client config within the resource that might be useful outside of the signature version, like actually accessing KMS encrypted objects using the API.

Is there any objection to adding a client argument to `boto3.resource()`/`boto3.session.resource()` or is this against some design principle of boto3?

issue_title: UWSGI process fails to respond after Harakiri/respawn
issue_body:
We run a django application under UWSGI.  We use harakiri to protect against stuck processes.  Any request that takes longer than 60 seconds will trigger a Harakiri in the worker process and the master uwsgi process will spawn a new child process.  This works very well when datadog is not installed.

When datadog is installed, however, uwsgi starts a new child process but that newly-spawned process never responds to any more requests.

Attached is an example from our logs.  Look for the word DAMN.  Please notice, activity for the new worker process that is started is absent from the log.  

[web1.log.txt](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/files/2103018/web1.log.txt)

After a while, this happens again, and then there are no more responding workers and the server becomes inaccessible for web requests.

If we remove datadog, we do not experience this problem.

Also attached is the configuration file we use for UWSGI.  We are using UWSGI 2.0.17
[django.ini.txt](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/files/2102943/django.ini.txt)

What could be going wrong?

issue_title: IOError on adding first submodule
issue_body:
If there is not already a .gitmodules file, attempting to add a submodule through GitPython produces an IOError.

Creating the first submodule is a perfectly normal operation, and as such should not be considered an error - the .gitmodules file should just be created.

issue_title: TChannel.advertise should be safe to call multiple times
issue_body: Currently, advertise for the sync and async clients do not keep track whether they're already advertising. This means that calling advertise multiple times is unsafe because that'll just start up multiple advertise loops. We should keep track of advertisement status so that it's safe to all advertise multiple times.

issue_title: Missing dependency on enum34 on python 2
issue_body:
Okonomiyaki now depends on `enum34`. That dependency was missed out because it is implicitly installed by haas when running tests.

@ashishbhate can you quickly tackle this ? Because `enum34` is only required on python 2 (we don't care about python 3 < 3.4), you should use extra requires (see e.g. https://github.com/enthought/jaguar/blob/master/setup.py#L39)
