issue_title: SQLAlchemy/Hive connect to Spark Thrift Server (based on HiveThriftServer2)
issue_body:
Hi, 

I was trying to connect to a Spark HiveThriftServer2 on Spark 2.1.0 and https://github.com/airbnb/superset with its SQLLab tool which is sqlalchemy based. While running queries with sqlalchemy works fine, superset uses among other things get_schema_names in sqlalchemy_hive.py. I realised that Spark returns a slightly different result for SHOW SCHEMAS. It uses databaseName instead of database_name as column name, the later being expected in sqlalchemy_hive.py.

I was able to make it work with a small modification which should work for Hive, too (see below) and I was wondering if there is a chance to bring that in?

```
def get_schema_names(self, connection, **kw):
        # Equivalent to SHOW DATABASES
        return [row[0] for row in connection.execute('SHOW SCHEMAS')]
```

There are a few other problems when superset tries to get table names (index of name different) and column definitions (with get_columns) for Spark temporary views, but that is because Spark does not seem to consistently work with schemas/dbs. For example 'SHOW TABLES IN myschema' would show the temporary view but 'DESCRIBE myschmea.myview' would produce an error. I haven't tested if it is the same for persisted tables. Anyway that is a spark bug.

I realise that you are probably not aiming to support the Spark Thrift server, but having this small change in would actually help me a lot and not break Hive compatibility. Should you be interested in implementing a sqlalchemy Spark dialect on the other hand, I'd be happy to get involved.

issue_title: Runtime outputs illegal type comments
issue_body:
During a profiling run, the runtime sometimes outputs type comments that are illegal. The resulting output JSON file cannot be used as the `--type-info` parameter of `pyannotate`, as the type comments cannot be successfully parsed.

This is using Python 3.6 and `pyannotate==1.0.2`; full repro below.

Here are a few examples of the invalid type comments that are generated, and the corresponding parse errors:
```
{
    "path": "venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pytz/__init__.py",
    "line": 126,
    "func_name": "timezone",
    "type_comments": [
        "(str) -> pytz.tzfile.Europe/Amsterdam",
        "(str) -> pytz.tzfile.US/Eastern"
    ],
    "samples": 2
}

pyannotate_tools.annotations.parse.ParseError: Invalid type comment: (str) -> pytz.tzfile.Europe/Amsterdam
---------------------
{
    "path": "venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pytz/tzfile.py",
    "line": 26,
    "func_name": "build_tzinfo",
    "type_comments": [
        "(str, _io.BufferedReader) -> pytz.tzfile.Europe/Amsterdam",
        "(str, _io.BufferedReader) -> pytz.tzfile.US/Eastern"
    ],
    "samples": 2
}

pyannotate_tools.annotations.parse.ParseError: Invalid type comment: (str, _io.BufferedReader) -> pytz.tzfile.Europe/Amsterdam
---------------------
{
    "path": "venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pytz/tzinfo.py",
    "line": 166,
    "func_name": "DstTzInfo.__init__",
    "type_comments": [
        "(Tuple[datetime.timedelta, datetime.timedelta, str], Dict[Tuple[datetime.timedelta, datetime.timedelta, str], pytz.tzfile.US/Eastern]) -> None",
        "(None, None) -> pyannotate_runtime.collect_types.UnknownType"
    ],
    "samples": 5
}

pyannotate_tools.annotations.parse.ParseError: Invalid type comment: (Tuple[datetime.timedelta, datetime.timedelta, str], Dict[Tuple[datetime.timedelta, datetime.timedelta, str], pytz.tzfile.US/Eastern]) -> None
```

Seems like the `/` in the `pytz` type name is causing the problem.

To reproduce:
- clone the GraphQL compiler project: https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler
- check out the master branch, make a Python 3.6 virtualenv, install the project dependencies
- `pip install pytest-annotate`, a plugin for `pytest` that will run PyAnnotate during tests
- `py.test --annotate-output ./annotations.json` which will output PyAnnotate annotations into `annotations.json`
- `pyannotate --type-info ./annotations.json ./graphql_compiler/compiler/compiler_frontend.py`, which will crash with the errors above

issue_title: Possible fix for ImportError: No module named django.core
issue_body:
I tried your installer in a virtualenv in Windows 7.  I kept getting the same error as this guy: https://github.com/nephila/djangocms-installer/issues/165

I ended up having to change one line in djangocms_installer\django\__init__.py

In the create_project function, I changed the syntax from:

subprocess.check_call(' '.join(['django-admin.py', 'startproject'] + args), shell=True)

to:

subprocess.check_call(' '.join(['python virtualenv_path\Scripts\django-admin.py', 'startproject'] + args), shell=True)

After that, the installation process went pretty smoothly.  Not sure how others have tackled this problem, but I was thinking that this might be helpful to others Windows users trying to install django cms.

Update: I'm using Python 2.7.8

issue_title: Question: What Environment variables are loaded by `pipenv shell`?
issue_body:
I'm using Visual Studio Code as an IDE in Windows. When I open an integrated terminal in vscode, it loads `cmd` in the root of the current project. I then type `pipenv shell` to load that project's environment before doing other terminal-ly things.

I would like to go directly to the pipenv shell when opening a terminal within a project, without having to type this command. My options are either:

1. Paste a different `.exe` path in this option, and add the `shell` argument as an argument
    ```
    // The path of the shell that the terminal uses on Windows. When using shells shipped with Windows (cmd, PowerShell or Bash on Ubuntu).
    "terminal.integrated.shell.windows": "C:\\Windows\\system32\\cmd.exe"
    ```

2. Add where to load whatever Windows environment variables get loaded by the `pipenv shell` command here
   ```
   // Object with environment variables that will be added to the VS Code process to be used by the terminal on Windows```

My question is where could I find either... value to input?

##### Describe your environment

1. Windows 7 SP 1
1. Python version: 3.5.2
1. Pipenv version: 8.3.2

issue_title: Prepare for Pip10
issue_body:
- [x] Vendor pip9 (dirty) (notpip)
- [x] Vendor pip9 (clean)
- [x] Rewrite all imports.

issue_title: Importing pkg_resources is slow. Work is done at import time.
issue_body:
Any "import pkg_resources" by a module is slow, in the 100-150ms range, depending on the system.

This is due to the number of modules imported by pkg_resources itself (email.parser is also slow), and side-effect work done at import time, notably the `_initialize` and `_initialize_master_working_set` functions at the bottom of `pkg_resources/__init__.py`.

As wall clock time matters to humans running CLIs, every millisecond counts.

The work done at import time should be deferred until needed, and the imports themselves also deferred if possible.

issue_title: Encoding problems with pkg_file on 2.7
issue_body:
In `dist.py`, the function `write_pkg_info` [specifies an encoding](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/dist.py#L79) when opening the `PKG-INFO` file for writes. However, the function `write_pkg_file` uses [string interpolation](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/dist.py#L44) when writing metadata, e.g. 

    file.write('Metadata-Version: %s\n' % version)

On 2.7, `'Metadata-Version: %s\n'` is a `str`, not `unicode`. If the Python package is 2/3 compatible, then the name, version, and especially data often read from files such as the license will be unicode strings. As such, they can raise a `UnicodeEncodeError` when encoded to the default 2.7 ascii encoding.

There is a test repository showing this behaviour here: https://github.com/cmutel/setuptools_encoding_pkg_file_bug

I can't think of a way to fix this without breaking existing code except for a wrapper function that would try to figure out the right thing to do with different types of strings (and different python versions). Note that this issue can occur any time there is string interpolation of data read from `setup.py`.

issue_title: iter_entry_point often spending a lot of time on parsing a dummy requirements string
issue_body:
When investigating a performance issue on a tool that makes use of setuptools entry points [1], profiling pointed at iter_entry_points as a hot point (see flamegraph attached to [1]), with iter_entry_points surprisingly spending a lot of time in pyparsing.

Digging further it appears that when an EntryPoint is created, even without anything in "extras", Requirements.parse is still called (see [2]) and ultimately results in parsing a dummy/empty requirements string ("x[]").

[1] https://bugs.launchpad.net/python-openstackclient/+bug/1702483
[2] https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L2289

issue_title: Doesn't work with single-user python3.5 on Windows
issue_body:
On windows 7 with Python 3.5 installed for a single user (in c:\users\username\appdata\local\programs\python\python35), virtualenv fails with the message:

"virtualenv is not compatible with this system or executable
Note: some Windows users have reported this error when they installed Python for "only this user" or have multiple versions of python installed.  Copying the appropriate pythonxx.dll to the virtualenv scripts directory may fix this problem."

So virtualenv understands that there is a problem, but misdiagnosed the cause.  The py35\Scripts directory contains python.exe, pythonw.exe, and python35.dll.  It does not contain activate.bat or any other files.  Not surprisingly, trying to run py35\python.exe directly without an activate fails with a missing VCRUNTIME140.dll.

The same installation of virtualenv created a py27 environment with no problems from c:\python27.

issue_title: Output on a pipe stops at 64K
issue_body:
On Linux, when the output of the `js-yaml` CLI is a pipe to another command, the output is truncated to the first 65536 bytes.

Here is how to reproduce (with any big enough YAML file):
```
$ wget http://static.snoyman.com/entries.yaml     # A 190K YAML file
$ js-yaml entries.yaml > entries.json ; wc -c entries.json
245736 entries.json
$ js-yaml entries.yaml | wc -c
65536
```

Tested with `node` v6.1.0  (arch: amd64) and `js-yaml` 3.6.0 with bash 4.3.11(1) on Ubuntu 14.04.

issue_title: Move API docs to autodocs/docstrings
issue_body:
Currently the API reference is listed manually in `usage.rst`, this makes it too easy to have it out of sync with the code (it is out of sync as of this moment, see: http://parsel.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#parsel.selector.SelectorList.__nonzero__).

Let's move it to docstrings and use autodocs.

issue_title: Getting latest available offset fails sporadically
issue_body:
Calling the `latest_available_offset()` method on a partition fails sporadically with this trace output:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/usr/local/tal/tal/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tal_kafka/scripts/consumer_group_lag.py", line 100, in get_consumer_lag
        topic_offset = pykafka_topic.partitions[partition].latest_available_offset()
      File "/usr/local/tal/tal/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/partition.py", line 111, in latest_available_offset
        return self.fetch_offset_limit(OffsetType.LATEST)[0]
    IndexError: list index out of range

This is in a script that runs once a minute or so, and this exception occurs infrequently, pointing to some transient issue with broker state perhaps.

As a workaround, I have wrapped the `latest_available_offset()` call in a retry loop. I'll post more information here if I notice anything helpful in the logs.

This is with pykafka 2.1.2, but looking at the latest code on the master branch, the code for this call seems unchanged.

issue_title: talk about usage of __ and _ vars in docs
simpleconsumer does not raise errors in fetch_offsets.
issue_body:
simpleconsumer is not correctly raising KafkaException in the fetch_offsets method. This is due to the following test: 
 https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L597-L598
```
        if len(parts_by_error) > 1:
            raise KafkaException(parts_by_error)
```
When the same error code is returned for all partitions, such as GroupLoadInProgress.ERROR, then the length of parts_by_error will be 1, preventing the raising of KafkaException. This results in the subsequent reseting of the partition's offsets when messages are consumed.

I'm working on incorporating the ``photutils.geometry`` routines into my code for creating weight matrices.  The routine ``circular_overlap_region`` works fine (i.e. maximum value is 1.0) for both exact and sampled cases. issue_title: Implement Jarvis March, Graham Scan and Chan's Algorithm
issue_body:
[Chan's Algorithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chan%27s_algorithm) is a very fast algorithm for computing convex hull of an image. It utilizes [Jarvis March](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gift_wrapping_algorithm) and [Graham Scan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graham_scan) to produce the result in optimal _O_(nlogh) time complexity. I would like to work on its implementation.

For this, I have few questions to ask,

1. Should we make all the three above mentioned algorithms publicly available in the API? Or just the Chan's Algorithm?
2. Should we replace the current implementation of `skimage.morphology.convex_hull_image` with Chan's Algorithm or add a separate function along with it? Or rather use `kwarg` to manage both of them?

Thank you.

- Windows 10 - 64 bit
- Python 3.5.1
- scikit-image 0.13.1

## Way to reproduce
_any image_ 

```
from skimage.segmentation import slic
from skimage.util import img_as_float
image = cv2.imread("test.png", 1)
n_segments = 50
compactness = 20.0
sigma = 3
max_iter = 10

slic_zero = 0
segments = slic(img_as_float(image), n_segments, compactness, sigma, max_iter, min_size_factor, max_size_factor, slic_zero, spacing=None)

issue_title: bug in rotate resize
issue_body:
There is a bug in rotate() if resize=True. The output image is not resized correctly.

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/953fbfc539217ad4c92fcf14a9d6dfe1b4b1b793/skimage/transform/_warps.py#L273
should be 
`translation = -np.asarray((minc, minr))`

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/953fbfc539217ad4c92fcf14a9d6dfe1b4b1b793/skimage/transform/_warps.py#L277
should be

Make computeRawAnomalyScore() smoother
issue_body:
There are 3 branches of `if` in `Anomaly.computeRawAnomalyScore()` - the core function compution anomalies. 

* the 1st branch is smooth with regards to #predicted & #active columns. 
* the branch "score = 0.0" is OK I guess
* I have a problem with the part "score = 1.0" 
https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/algorithms/anomaly.py#L47-L50 
This creates a `big` anomaly, without taking #columns into account. 
  * 0 (zero) active cols at T & 1 (out of 10.000, say) predicted col at T-1 creates the same anomaly as 
  * 0 active cols at T & 50% (out of 10.000, say) predicted cols at T-1

issue_title: Remove timestamp, metric value from anomaly likelihood code
issue_body:
Currently the likelihood code takes the timestamp and metric value for each record:
https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/76e5b07b35764826b34221104e9c43704138b9e3/src/nupic/algorithms/anomaly_likelihood.py#L176

As far as I can tell, the timestamp is not used and the metric value is used solely for determining whether the metric is flat, which isn't handled well according to this code:
https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/76e5b07b35764826b34221104e9c43704138b9e3/src/nupic/algorithms/anomaly_likelihood.py#L356-L367

I propose that we:

- Remove the timestamp
- If possible, move the logic that checks for a flat metric value outside the AnomalyLikelihood class. It may make more sense to do this logic when determining the raw anomaly score since it is intended to correct a mistake in the temporal memory and raw score calculation, not in the likelihood calculation.

@subutai - thoughts?
CC @andrewmalta13 re: #3220

issue_title: Bug in adding synapses in temporal_memory
issue_body:
The mechanism for adding synapses to a segment in temporal_memory has a subtle bug. When learning on a segment `self.maxNewSynapseCount - len(activeSynapses)` new synapses are added.  

This logic doesn't allow you to learn unions. Instead it should always try to add self.maxNewSynapseCount new synapses as long as you don't get over maxSynapsesPerSegment. The logic should be something like `min(self.maxNewSynapseCount, maxSynapsesPerSegment - numSynapsesOnSegment)`.

A slightly different alternate is to add self.maxNewSynapseCount new synapses and then, if you are over maxSynapsesPerSegment you can remove least used or lowest permanence synapses to make room. 

Making this change requires some testing to make sure we don't unwittingly lower performance on existing benchmarks like hotgym, taxi and NAB.  The change would need to be made both on CPP and Python versions.

issue_title: "pycapnp==0.5.5" is not in `bindings/py/requirements.txt`
issue_body:
README.md states:

> The Python dependencies (NumPy and pycapnp) can be installed with pip:
> 
> ```
> pip install -r bindings/py/requirements.txt
> ```

But capnp is not indeed in `bindings/py/requirements.txt`, instead, it's installed by `extra_require` in `setup.py`:

```python
  setup(
    name="nupic.bindings",
    version=getVersion(),
    # This distribution contains platform-specific C++ libraries, but they are not
    # built with distutils. So we must create a dummy Extension object so when we
    # create a binary file it knows to make it platform-specific.
    ext_modules=[Extension('nupic.dummy', sources = ['dummy.c'])], 
    namespace_packages=["nupic"],
    install_requires=findRequirements(platform),
    packages=find_packages(),
    package_data={
        "nupic.proto": ["*.capnp"],
        "nupic.bindings": ["*.so", "*.pyd"],
    },
    extras_require = {"capnp": ["pycapnp==0.5.5"]},
```

Is this intended? Why?

This causes the following 2 problems:

* A new user would fail if only goes "Building from Source -> Prerequisites" to "Developer Installation -> Configure and generate C++ build files" on a whole new environment
* inconsistency between documentation and actual code

issue_title: WebSearch: XML output and split-by-collection
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-03-08

Indico Search calls Invenio instance and gets results in MARCXML.  The search is being called twice, once for collection Foo and once for collection Bar.  In order to speed up the queries, it would be good to enrich XML output with comments specifying results split by collection.  This will necessitate only one search call from Indico.

Using Invenio demo site as an exapmle, for a query like `http://invenio-demo.cern.ch/search?p=nuclear&of=xm&sc=1`, the system can reply in enriched markup e.g. using XML comments like:

```
[...]
<!-- Search-Engine-Total-Number-Of-Results: 7 -->
<!-- Search-Engine-Collection: Articles and Preprints -->
<!-- Search-Engine-Collection-Number-Of-Results: 6 -->
<record>
  <controlfield tag="001">65</controlfield>
  [...]
</record>
[...]
<!-- Search-Engine-Collection: Books and Reports -->
<!-- Search-Engine-Collection-Number-Of-Results: 1 -->
<record>
  <controlfield tag="001">32</controlfield>
</record>
```

See also #363.

issue_title: tests: simplify doctest execution
issue_labels: [{'id': 414887011, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-db/labels/Size:%20easy', 'name': 'Size: easy', 'color': 'bc3cbc', 'default': False}, {'id': 414886980, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-db/labels/Status:%20ready%20for%20work', 'name': 'Status: ready for work', 'color': '149414', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: global: release checklist step 1-3
Pure-Python Wrapper objects break `object.__getattribute__` in methods of the wrapped object
issue_body:
Certain "base" classes make direct use of `object.__getattribute__` in their method implementations. (i.e., *outside* of their own `__getattribute__` implementation). They may do this for performance reasons, or to simplify their implementation of `__getattribute__`.  One prominent example is `persistent.Persistent`, but there are undoubtedly others.

If classes like this are wrapped with a pure-Python acquisition wrapper, those methods fail, because the `self` they have is not the `self` they expect (it's the wrapper), and directly calling `object.__getattribute__` bypasses the wrapper's `__getattribute__` implementation.

Here's an example test that fails with pure-Python, but works under the C implementation:

```python
    def test_object_getattribute_in_rebound_method(self):

        class Persistent(object):
            __slots__ = ('__flags')
            def __init__(self):
                self.__flags = 42

            def get_flags(self):
                return object.__getattribute__(self, '_Persistent__flags')

        wrapped = Persistent()
        wrapper = Acquisition.ImplicitAcquisitionWrapper(wrapped, None)

        self.assertEqual(wrapped.get_flags(), wrapper.get_flags())
```

It fails like so:
```
  File "/Acquisition/tests.py", line 3171, in test_object_getattribute_in_rebound_method
    self.assertEqual(wrapped.get_flags(), wrapper.get_flags())
  File "/Acquisition/tests.py", line 3166, in get_flags
    return object.__getattribute__(self, '_Persistent__flags')
AttributeError: 'ImplicitAcquisitionWrapper' object has no attribute '_Persistent__flags'
```

I'm trying to figure out how to fix this.

issue_title: OrderedContainer uses the wrong name in `self._order` and rejects ascii keys under Python 3
issue_body:
It uses `z.c.contained.setitem()` to put the data in `self._data`. That function will decode a `bytes` key if needed before storing it with the provided `__setitem__` function. But OrderedContainer stores the original value, not the decoded value. When you then go to delete the item, it removes it from `_order` by the key that you provide. 

This is more-or-less fine on Python 2 because of the implicit conversions between bytes and unicode---assuming the default locale stays the same everywhere:
```pycon
>>> from zope.container.ordered import OrderedContainer
>>> oc = OrderedContainer()
>>> oc[b'abc'] = 1
>>> del oc[u'abc']
```

On Python 3 there is an explicit pre-check that prevents bytes from even getting into `setitem`:
```pycon
>>> from zope.container.ordered import OrderedContainer
>>> oc = OrderedContainer()
>>> oc[b'abc'] = 1
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "//.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/zope/container/ordered.py", line 201, in __setitem__
    "ascii or unicode string" % key)
TypeError: 'b'abc'' is invalid, the key must be an ascii or unicode string
```

This pre-check is not actually tested. If we remove the pre-check so that `setitem` can do its decoding thing, then we can't remove the data:
```
>>> from zope.container.ordered import OrderedContainer
>>> oc = OrderedContainer()
>>> oc[b'abc'] = 1
>>> del oc[u'abc']
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "//.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/zope/container/ordered.py", line 244, in __delitem__
    self._order.remove(key)
  File "//.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/persistent/list.py", line 84, in remove
    self.__super_remove(item)
  File "//.tox/py36/lib/python3.6/collections/__init__.py", line 1084, in remove
    def remove(self, item): self.data.remove(item)
ValueError: list.remove(x): x not in list
```

This is a pretty easy fix

issue_title: Feature Request: APK Job Embedding
issue_body:
I am aware that this is likley a VERY large request but I feel, were it implemented, this would make objection truly one-of-a-kind.

My idea would be to allow startup jobs to be specified whilst patching an APK that would be ran when the app is opened. This would allow the app to function fully autonomously without the need for a connection to a computer.

This would be especially useful for the sslpinning job as I don't think there is a tool out there that offers this capability to be fully automated.

Frida [does allow](https://www.frida.re/docs/gadget/#script) a gadget to execute a script / scripts instead of waiting for a connection however am aware that a lot is done on the side of the PC, not Android device.

Again, I am aware of the magnitude of this request and would be shocked if it were to be implemented any time soon but I and i'm sure many other people would be glad if it were to be in the future.

Thanks,
Joseph.

issue_title: Add tests for Jinja + more for various options
FieldError when using custom user model
issue_body:
Django docs suggest to inherit custom user model from AbstractBaseUser class:
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/auth/customizing/#specifying-a-custom-user-model

AbstractBaseUser has attribute is_active that is True by default (it's not a database field)
https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/auth/models.py#L199

In this case django-rest-framework-jwt throws an error "FieldError: Cannot resolve keyword 'is_active' into field." when trying to do authentication:
https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/blob/deea4ad14dfd4611fb19ad3cab133b8dd3e8959c/rest_framework_jwt/authentication.py#L55

Also token verifycation and refresh fail:
https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/blob/faa772c53efe7123776206fa0513d597c5e2c368/rest_framework_jwt/serializers.py#L115

issue_title: Use 419 HTTP response for token timeout / configurable exception
issue_body:
It would be useful to have a more explicit status code in case the JWT token has expired, and it looks like "419 " would fit (via [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes#4xx_Client_Error)):

> 419 Authentication Timeout (not in RFC 2616)
    Not a part of the HTTP standard, 419 Authentication Timeout denotes that previously valid authentication has expired. It is used as an alternative to 401 Unauthorized in order to differentiate from otherwise authenticated clients being denied access to specific server resources.

This would help to distinguish between the other(s) 401's, instead of solely relying on the body's response text.

This might be achieved by using a new exception class for this:

    class AuthenticationExpired(APIException):
        status_code = 419
        default_detail = _('Signature has expired.')

Where normally the following class from `rest_framework.exceptions` is used:

    class AuthenticationFailed(APIException):
        status_code = status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED
        default_detail = _('Incorrect authentication credentials.')

There is no constant for 419 in `rest_framework.status` yet.
If we would agree to change this, this might get submitted to them additionally.

To be backward-compatible this behavior should be made configurable probably, possibly by allowing to configure what exception class to use.

Please let me know what you think about it, and I'd be happy to provide a PR for it.

issue_title: [Snippet Client] Backward compatibility mechanism with older versions of snippet lib
issue_body: If we make updates to snippet lib and introduce new internal Rpcs or other protocol changes, the client needs to be able to figure out which version of the lib is used and behave accordingly.

issue_title: Create a Snippet covering basic Android operations
issue_body:
Operations like toggling Wi-Fi/BT, connecting to a Wi-Fi network etc.

This way users can have an easy example to start with, as opposed to having to deal with the shenanigans of SL4A.

To do so, we'll need to create a new repo to host the code for the snippet implementation first.

issue_title: Issues uncovered by Coverity Scan
issue_body:
This is with revision d811b186c5037b434d56ddb831ceccdf5a954687.  I can't really reproduce the full report here, but here is a summary of each item it found.

* `enc/histogram.h:37`: Non-static class member `bit_cost_` is not initialized in this constructor nor in any functions that it calls.
* `enc/command.h:106`: Non-static class members `insert_len_`, `copy_len_`, `cmd_prefix_`, `dist_prefix_`, `cmd_extra_`, and `dist_extra_` are not initialized in this constructor nor in any functions that it calls.
* `enc/brotli_bit_stream.cc:343`: `index` is passed to a parameter that cannot be negative. *IndexOf can return -1*
* `enc/literal_cost.cc:102`: Execution cannot reach the expression 0 inside this statement: `last_c = ((i + window_half …`
  * at_least: At condition `i + window_half - 2 < 0`, the value of `i` must be at least 0.
   * const: At condition `i + window_half - 2 < 0`, the value of `window_half` must be equal to 495.
   * dead_error_condition: The condition `i + window_half - 2 < 0` cannot be true.
* `enc/prefix.h:75`: In expression `distance_code >> bucket`, shifting by a negative amount has undefined behavior. The shift amount, `bucket`, is -2.
  * return_constant: Function call `brotli::Log2Floor(distance_code)` returns -1.
  * assignment: Assigning: `bucket = brotli::Log2Floor(distance_code) - 1`. The value of `bucket` is now -2.
* `enc/entropy_encode.cc:45`: Non-static class members `total_count_`, `index_left_`, and `index_right_or_value_` are not initialized in this constructor nor in any functions that it calls.
* `enc/backward_references.cc:157`: The compiler-generated constructor for this class does not initialize `min_cost_cmd_`
* `enc/encode.cc:207`: Non-static class member `literal_cost_mask_` is not initialized in this constructor nor in any functions that it calls.
* `dec/huffman.c:142`: Using uninitialized value `sorted[symbol++]` ![dec/huffman.c:142](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1151/9540204/a5da6576-4d10-11e5-9b0d-70f8dc1f1438.png)
* `dec/decode.c:871`: Using uninitialized value `s.loop_counter` when calling `BrotliDecompressStreaming`
* `enc/metablock.cc:496`: Using tainted variable context as an index to pointer `static_context_map`
`enc/static_dict.cc:391`: `data[0] == '\xc2'` is always false regardless of the values of its operands. This occurs as the logical operand of if.

If you need more details about an item let me know.  I only provided the full information on the one item because it is a bit difficult to follow without the it, but I think the others should be pretty easy to figure out.

issue_title: What's the best way to fix the failing Travis build?
issue_body:
I'm looking to add support for another geocoding service to Geopy, but I'm kind of afraid to do it until the master project is passing its CI tests with Travis.

For me, It's that I want my PR to be accepted without a problem, and not passing all the tests is a surefire way to ensure my PR never gets merged.

But at the same time, I also love the Geopy project (been building tools with Geopy long before I started working on a geocoder project) and don't want to scare folks away from adopting it and instead toward more vendor-specific implementations.

So what's the best way forward?

issue_title: yaml in unit tests
Chromatic Real Galaxy
issue_body:
GalSim has both `RealGalaxy` objects, and `ChromaticObject`s, but doesn't have `ChromaticRealGalaxy` (yet!).

@rmjarvis, @rmandelb, and I talked a bit about how one might take high-res HST images of a galaxy through 2+ filters, deconvolve the known (chromatic) HST PSF, and then reconvolve by a different chromatic PSF (to presumably less resolution than HST, c.f. `RealGalaxy`).  This would be especially helpful for studies of galaxies with color-gradients for LSST/Euclid/etc.

[Semboloni++13](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2013MNRAS.432.2385S) section 5.2 equation 25 briefly lays this out for the case where the SED at each point in the galaxy is linear in wavelength.  We think we can generalize this to the case of generic SEDs, for instance, typical bulge and disk SEDs.

This issue is to develop the framework for a `ChromaticRealGalaxy` object.  For the moment, I think it's reasonable to limit the scope to just the framework.  In particular, this issue will not attempt to actually assemble a catalog of HST images and PSFs; we'll do that later, and in the meantime we can test with simulated GalSim images.

issue_title: Icon request: icon-accessible
issue_body:
One group, the Accessible Icon Project, has created a new logo that they hope will ultimately replace ISO standard. New York City has recently adopted this revised wheelchair logo.
http://99percentinvisible.org/episode/icon-for-access/
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/13696875/9140347/1ab881e6-3cf6-11e5-8d85-9df7088d968c.png)


1. Head is forward to indicate the forward motion of the person through space. Here the person is the “driver” or decision maker about her mobility.

2. Arm angle is  pointing backward to suggest the dynamic mobility of a chair user, regardless of whether or not she uses her arms. Depicting the body in motion represents the symbolically active status of navigating the world.

3. By including white angled knockouts the symbol presents the wheel as being in motion. These knockouts also work for creating stencils used in spray paint application of the icon. Having just one version of the logo keeps things more consistent and allows viewers to more clearly understand intended message.

4. The human depiction in this icon is consistent with other body representations found in the ISO 7001 – DOT Pictograms. Using a different portrayal of the human body would clash with these established and widely used icons and could lead to confusion.

5. The leg has been moved forward to allow for more space between it and the wheel which allows for better readability and cleaner application of icon as a stencil.

–Accessible Icon Project

issue_title: needs tests, coverage, build badges
Security issue with guake --execute-command
issue_body: If you have inside guake tab root shell or a shell on remote server, any other program can run anything there. For security reasons `--execute-command` should be allowed only if shell in selected tab is not running any other programs, like ssh or sudo.

issue_title: Way to Start Guake With Tab Set

When I start up Guake I always do the following:

1. start my API server
2. open a new tab, start my web server
3. open a new tab, start my webpack dev server
4. open a new tab, cd in to my application's directory

It would be really nice if, instead of having to type `start-api-server`, `CTRL`+`SHIFT`+`T`, `start-web-server`, ... I could somehow just tell Guake "start with a tab that runs `start-api-server`, a tab that runs `start-web-server`, etc.

This could be extremely minimal, because as long as there is *some* way to start with multiple tabs open, and have them each run a single command, users can customize that one command to run whatever other commands they want (by making it an alias or shell script).

issue_title: Different results between calculate and simulate endpoints
issue_body:
There is a difference between `calculate` and `simulate` endpoints for the same test case.

The next step to solve this issue is to develop a [decomposition](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/next/openfisca_france/decompositions/decomp.xml) checker which guarantees that the sums of the aggregated nodes equals the calculated variable with the same name.

For exemple the [`psoc`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/a3772aef8b5e58876205b6c0bf30b1fcb888c06b/openfisca_france/decompositions/decomp.xml#L83-L116) node aggregates 3 sub-nodes (`pfam`, `mini` and `aides_logement`), and the variable [`psoc`](http://legislation.openfisca.fr/variables/psoc) sums the same 3 variables.

That's correct but this could have been wrong if `decomp.xml` was false.

issue_title: null column values in styling not handled correctly
issue_body:
Dataset is earthquakes and styling for both color and size is ramp of magnitude (5 bins, equal interval). Null values in the column used for styling are being colored as though they are in the highest color bin using CARTOframes.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12551177/32517488-9956e03a-c3d4-11e7-9786-9e4b60c096e1.png)

Equivalent styling in CARTO Builder shows that the null values default to the lowest color in the ramp.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12551177/32517628-0152530e-c3d5-11e7-91ea-d70f1340be9f.png)

We should change this so the null values correspond to the lowest value of ramp, the same as in Builder.

issue_title: consider using hupper for reloading
issue_body:
Hey folks, I've done a lot of work in Pyramid's pserve (which of course is derived from PasteScript -
 the same code as gearbox's serve command) to make the reloader smarter. One major feature is that it does not crash when your code fails to start, so you do not need to continually restart it. The library that does this was pulled out into a framework-agnostic entity called hupper.

http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/hupper/en/latest/

Anyway, I just wanted to bring it to your attention incase you are interested in using it for an improved `--reload` experience.

issue_title: Deploying under the same API Gateway ID
issue_body:
Hi,

So i started being around with chalice and started to get more organized at the way i'm distributing the codes for my API. To do so i've created 2 folders: One to hold the create (POST) calls and the other one for the research/query (GET) calls.

The first thing i expected was to see the create (POST) call creating a brand new API Gateway instance because the project folder was freshly created. With that in my i proceed to deploy the code, wait to finish, then i edited the `.chalice/deployed.json`: changed the `rest_api_id` entry so both project (POST and GET) could be deployed under the same API Gateway instance.

For my surprise after triggering the deployment again on the create (POST) project my old and already deployed/linked research (GET) call was removed from the API Gateway leaving on the create (POST) available there.

So my question is the following:

> With Chalice as your framework you will be obligated to concentrated into a single project folder all the calls you might want to server under the same API Gateway instance?

Like, i know we have the `chalicelib` to handle extra files beside the `app.py` but i don't think it's a good ideia to put like 20 calls into a single project like this. Right?

Or is better to have all of them on separated API Gateways and unified them later through a Route 53 configuration?

issue_title: Exceptions raised from some event handlers freeze the analysis
Incorrect workspace creation
issue_body:
Currently, when a workspace isn't specified, Manticore creates two workspaces on the filesystem. This is due to [manticore/manticore.py:708](https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/master/manticore/manticore.py#L708), as `ws_path` is `None`.

The fix is two fold: 

1. Have `Workspace.uri` return a full, type-qualified path (i.e. `fs:/path`), and
2. Pass the uri to the `Executor`

issue_title: NEXRAD files with non-uniform gate locations
issue_body:
NEXRAD files can contain volumes where the gate spacing and gate locations are not constant between sweeps.  When these file are read, radar.range is set to the gate spacing for the sweep with the largest number of gates.  These results in some sweeps having an incorrect range.  An example file which displays this property is KMLB20101118_014806_V03 which is available from the NCDC.

File exhibiting this behavior should at the least detected and a warning/error raised. If possible find a method for returning all or some of the sweeps with the correct gate spacing (multiple radar objects?).

Thanks to Timoth Lang for reporting this bug.

issue_title: Question about routine radar_coords_to_cart in multi-radar gridding
issue_body:
Recently I reviewed the procedure of multi-radar gridding and find something might not be accurate in `grid_mapper`. If my understanding is correct, `map_to_grid` function in `grid_mapper` module iterate a list of radars, apply `radar_coords_to_cart` for each sweep to obtain a `(x, y, z)` for each gate. However, the `radar_coords_to_cart` function is a radar-station centric function. This means `azimuth==0` indicates the true north thus it is along the Y-axis. It is OK in any single radar mapping but in any multi-radar mapping, as long as two and more radar (especially they're far away)

Consider an unreal corner case like this. If two radar are located near to north pole: Radar A is (88.9N, 90E), Radar B is (88.9N, 90W). If mapping together, it would be a wrong to use `radar_coords_to_cart` individually and directly mosaick them using calculated (x, y): actually y_a and y_b should on same line but current routine in `grid_mapper` probably produces two parallel lines for y_a and y_b but put x_a and x_b on a same line. 

To correct this "rotating" issue, I got two ways now I could think out to solve this (if it is an issue). One way is plot multi-radar grids in a geographically projected map, not a lat-lon or unprojected planar map, then plus the geodesic convergence angle at the radar station to all azimuth recored; the second way is in `radar_coords_to_cart`, the finally result is converted back to lat-lon then all final results are plotted in lat-lon coordinates. The previous one needs the a calculation of geodesic convergence angle but I cannot figure out how to do this in proj.4 lib (unless we use a conic projection). The second way needs a lot of conversion from x-y to lat-lon along the great circle (might cause performance issue). Probably an another way would be completely change the way to calculate range gate using the equation given by Doviak and Zrnic in 1993. However, that could be more code to add.

I am not sure if it is considered in other procedure so the radar azimuth is actually corrected by geodesic convergence angle. If so, could you be kind to let me know the branch/file. I find this problem in my other research so I came back to find some references here but failed.

Thanks

issue_title: Allow skipping and removing result during a check
issue_body:
trigger_update cannot distinguish between attributes of the model.

Sometimes you want to skip models with certain attributes or delete old results if a check ends with a specific value.

issue_title: add dispatch_uid to post_save connect
issue_body: will make disabling the signal during tests a lot easier

issue_title: Explore if it is possible to have test dependencies
issue_body: This would relate only to the 'checks' run on the models i.e. memote.memote.suite.tests, not to the tests that test the functionality of our code i.e. memote.tests.test_for_support

issue_title: Customizable color code
issue_body: The generated `index.html` produces color-coded displays, where the left-hand side scales from red to green, and the right-hand-side is blue. While I like this scheme, I still suggest making the color scheme customizable to support color-blind users.

issue_title: [tx] Fails to dump or convert a CFF2 font if it is not a variable font.
issue_body: A CFF2 font can be a non-variable font, in which case the length of the VariationStore table is 0. tx did not check if the length was 0 before trying to read the table.

issue_title: bug in addLigCaretEntryGDEF() in makeotf's hotconv/GDEF.c code
Add License
Add preemptive authentication
issue_body: I'd like to use opportunistic authentication on the server end and simply check for WWW-Authenticate header presence. The main idea is to prevent the first HTTP request which will always return 401 and instead supply the Kerberos credentials immideately. At first glance it seems to be pretty easy to implement on the client side.

issue_title: python3 and kerberos library
issue_body:
The kerberos library appears essentially abandonded. Amongst other things there will be no python 3.

We should transition the library to use python-gssapi which is a higher level wrapper that uses cffi and is actively maitained. 

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/python-gssapi/
https://github.com/sigmaris/python-gssapi

issue_title: Adopt common timeout/retry/backoff strategy for system tests.
issue_body:
From https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/1609#discussion_r56220537

We have a number of sporadic system test failures (#1085, #1089, #1100, #1104) due to "eventual consistency" in the tested APIs.  We have adopted a set of ad-hoc (and obviously not 100% reliable) strategies for working around the delays involved.

The point of this issue is to  adopt a consistent policy / mechanism across all system tests for addressing delays caused by eventual consistency.

@jonparrott can you comment here, collecting your thoughts expressed elsewhere?

issue_title: gcloud._helpers._compute_engine_id should return a str (Python 3)
issue_body:
From https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-datastore/issues/92:

I stumbled upon this while deploying my app for the first time on GKE/GCE.

I had to explicitly specify `project` when creating a client: `gcloud.datastore.Client(project="my-project")`, otherwise I just got an `gcloud.exceptions.ServiceUnavailable: 503 Backend Error`.

First I thought the problem would be that autodetecting the project-id didn't work, but `gcloud.datastore.Client(credentials=credentials, project=project)` returns my project-id, be it as `bytes` instead of `str`.

I quickly verified that using `bytes` as a project-id does not work, i.e. `gcloud.datastore.Client(project=b"my-project")` resulted in that neat `503 Backend Error`.

issue_title: storage: cannot access / delete versioned objects
issue_body:
Currently, `blob.delete` and `bucket.delete_blob` always delete the live version on buckets with versioning enabled. Other than being a missing feature, this can also cause some very unwanted behaviour, if a new version of the object is uploaded during the process of getting and deleting an object.

``` Diff
--- a/blob.py
+++ b/blob.py
@@ -279,7 +279,8 @@
                  (propagated from
                  :meth:`gcloud.storage.bucket.Bucket.delete_blob`).
         """
-        return self.bucket.delete_blob(self.name, client=client)
+        return self.bucket.delete_blob(self.name, self.generation, 
+            client=client)

     def download_to_file(self, file_obj, encryption_key=None, client=None):
         """Download the contents of this blob into a file-like object.
--- a/bucket.py
+++ b/bucket.py
@@ -357,7 +357,7 @@
         client.connection.api_request(method='DELETE', path=self.path,
                                       _target_object=None)

-    def delete_blob(self, blob_name, client=None):
+    def delete_blob(self, blob_name, generation=None, client=None):
         """Deletes a blob from the current bucket.

         If the blob isn't found (backend 404), raises a
@@ -392,6 +392,8 @@
         """
         client = self._require_client(client)
         blob_path = Blob.path_helper(self.path, blob_name)
+        if generation:
+            blob_path += '?generation=%i' % generation
         # We intentionally pass `_target_object=None` since a DELETE
         # request has no response value (whether in a standard request or
         # in a batch request).
```

issue_title: NTP client flexibility: please loosen Depends field for google-compute-engine deb
issue_body:
GCE best practice requires that VMs sync time via NTP to metadata.google.internal, but doesn't inherently require the particular NTP software in the "ntp" pacakge. Debian ships three NTP clients, and https://lwn.net/Articles/735211/ indicates that the more lightweight option chrony is the most secure.

Therefore, it would be great if you could loosen the mention of "ntp" in the google-compute-engine Depends line to indicate "chrony | ntp | time-daemon" instead. This should allow all available options to work, keeping whichever one is already installed upon upgrades, and preferring chrony if none are installed.

Thanks!

``` python
ts[end] = ts[end]   # freezing/anchoring the current value of ts as of [end, ...)
ts[start] = value      # changing the value as of [start, ...)
```

may fail as intermediate points in [start,end) may exist ==> we need to remove all intermediate points (which is easy as `ts.iterperiods(start,end)` provides them nicely).

I think the function below does it properly (but it would be better integrated in the **item** to use the slice notation)

``` python
def set_slice(ts, start, end, value):
    """
   ts[start:end] = value ==> call set_slice(ts, start, end, value)
    Set the value of the ts so that
    - on the interval [start, end) we have the new value
    - on [end, ...) we haven't change the value
    - on (..., start) we haven't change the value neither
    We replace the value of the ts on an interval.

    :param ts: 
    :param start: 
    :param end: 
    :param value: 
    :return: 
    """
    # for each interval to render
    for i, (s, e, v) in enumerate(list(ts.iterperiods(start, end))):
        # look at all intervals included in the current interval
        # (always at least 1)
        if i == 0:
            # if the first, set initial value to new value of range
            ts[s] = value
        else:
            # otherwise, remove intermediate key
            del ts[s]
    # finish by setting the end of the interval to the previous value
    ts[end] = v


```

issue_title: Dynamic importing fails inside PEX file
issue_body:
TL;DR: I cannot dynamically import submodules inside a PEX file because my discovery function is looking at the local filesystem instead of in the folders inside the PEX.

Long version: I am managing a project that consists of a central CLI framework we would like to distribute as a PEX. The CLI's commands are all submodules that are managed independently of the central project.

As I have no control over the commands, they are loaded dynamically as follows:

```python
def load_commands(plugin_folder, group):
    for folder in [x for x in os.listdir(plugin_folder) if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(plugin_folder, x))]:
        for fname in [x for x in os.listdir(os.path.join(plugin_folder, folder)) if x.endswith('_main.py')]:
            name = fname[:-8]
            try:
                module = __import__('.'.join(('my_project', 'commands', name, fname[:-3])), None, None, [name])
                cmd = getattr(module, name)
                group.add_command(cmd)
            except ImportError as e:
                announce_import_problem(e)
```

This works fine in an unpackaged distribution. But as soon as we try to run from inside a PEX, we get this:

`OSError: [Errno 20] Not a directory: '/Users/MyUsername/dist/my_pex.pex/my_project/commands'`

issue_title: Zinc doesn't invalidate on removal of required transitive type
issue_body:
Say you have 3 compilation units that declare a trait hierarchy:
```scala
trait Root
```

```scala
trait Middle extends Root
```

```scala
trait Leaf extends Middle
```

If you compile Leaf with both Middle and Root on the compile classpath, then compile it again with just Middle, the second compile will succeed, even though it should fail.

It should fail because scalac needs the transitive dependency, Root, on the classpath in order to compile Leaf against Middle. But it doesn't fail. Indeed if you do a clean compile without analysis, it will fail.

I think that this happens because Zinc only includes types in analysis that are actually used directly by the code being compiled. That Zinc doesn't include required transitive types in its analysis, and so ignores them not being present.


This becomes an issue for pants projects when incremental compile is enabled.

issue_body:
During a fix for a bug found, John suggested the following:
https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2697/

It would be nice if the register calls were the single source of knowledge of plugins - right now you have to repeat them all in the `global_subsystems` impl.  It may be worth the tradeoff of a class level registry / register method if all the register calls are made at the bottom of this file.  That would work with 3rdparty registrations too, they'd depend on the python backend and call the register classmethod in their register.py.  The solution right now does avoid mutable global state, but it also doesn;t actually allow plugins for real, just the hardocded internal ones you have now.

issue_title: Bundle behaviour change for directories in v2
issue_body:
The `v2` engine currently captures matched directories in `Snapshot`s, but doesn't actually propagate them through to `FilesetWithSpec`. One effect of this is the `jvm_app` `bundles` that explicitly refer to directories don't actually end up recursively including the directory content.

The `fileset` args here are matching directories, rather than files, and the semantics of `fileset` are ill defined. The following bundle fileset:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/')
```
...will not match any files in v2. But changing the glob to:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/**/*')
```
...will recursively match all files under the directory, similar to the v1 engine.

----

I think there are two changes to make here.

1. We should include all matched paths in the FilesetWithSpec in order to support the existing bundle case, and because it is completely reasonable for `tasks`/`Rules` to be able to capture directories.
2. We should add a deprecation warning for bundles for the case where a bundle matches _only_  directories. The idea is not to ban matching directories in the long term (that's totally reasonable)... instead, the goal would be to try to detect cases where people were expecting everything under a directory to be recursively included by a `fileset`, and instead ask them to change to an explicit recursive glob if that's what they wanted.

issue_title: Port BaseTest from MutableBuildGraph to LegacyBuildGraph
issue_body:
`tests/python/pants_test/base_test.py` creates a `MutableBuildGraph` and corresponding `BuildFileAddressMapper`, so all _unit_ tests continue to use the v1 pathways (mostly). This was discovered in #4376, which had adequate unit test coverage, but no integration coverage.

Because `BaseTest` allows a test to configure a custom set of `BuildFileAliases` (ie, symboltable), fixing this will involve fixing the fact that `LegacySymbolTable` uses an escape hatch to get at a static copy of the aliases.

issue_title: Add a platform constraints feature to python_{library, dist} targets
issue_body:
Add support for the following use cases:

1) I have a python_requirement that is Linux-only and want to include it in a library that I maintain. It would be better for a clear error message indicating incompatible platforms than an opaque resolver error that requires the user to have knowledge of the python-repos in play and hidden details of the library/requirement's platform compatibility. Furthermore, the linter will fail currently if one tries to build a binary on osx due the the lint goal's dependency on pyprep (due to changes introduced in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5765), and this will cause lint runs to fail. The new feature I am proposing in this ticket will fix this in the interim while the long term solution for linting is under development (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5884).

2) I maintain a python_dist that contains platform-specific code and hence dependees can only build, test and run for the "current" platform. I would like to de-cruft the python_binary targets by not requiring authors to specify `platforms=("current")`, and take the responsibility off of the user for knowing to include this, potentially saving time and headache while debugging. This is useful because unlike (1), a python_dist does not need to be constrained to any platform in particular, and incompatibilities due to "this os only" requirements should be caught per the use case described in (1).

I propose a new parameter for python{library, dist, requirement_library} targets (so essentially on the `PythonTarget` object): a `platform_constraints` parameter that accepts platforms strings in the exact same manner as `platforms` on the binary target. The running tasks would merge these constraints with the constraints found elsewhere and error out with a useful message if incompatibilities exist.

issue_title: Street name is parsed to street name
issue_body:
Meadow is a street mode (see [this page](http://pe.usps.gov/text/pub28/28apc_002.htm)). But it's parsed to street name. See below
```
>>> usaddress.parse('3054 Hidden Meadow, Seguin, TX 78155')
[(u'3054', 'AddressNumber'), (u'Hidden', 'StreetName'), (u'Meadow,', 'StreetName'), (u'Seguin,', 'PlaceName'), (u'TX', 'StateName'), (u'78155', 'ZipCode')]
```

issue_title: 3121 187th street crt n east moline il 61244 unit test is incorrect
issue_body:
I am working at adding additional STREET_NAMES in the usaddress/__init__.py.  One of the STREET_NAMES I added was 'crt'.  Unfortunately, this caused one of the unit tests to fail.  

This is an unusual address, for certain, but I *think* it should be tagged such that "187th Street" is the street name, and StreetNamePostType is 'crt'.  The reason I believe this to be true is that streets should generally not have multiple StreetNamePostDirectionals.  Obviously, there are going to be exceptions to this rule, but the general training should take care of this.


`FAIL: test_labeling.TestPerformance.test_all(b'3121 187th street crt n east moline il 61244', ('AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'StreetNamePostDirectional', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode'), ('AddressNumber', 'StreetName', 'StreetNamePostType', 'StreetNamePostType', 'StreetNamePostDirectional', 'PlaceName', 'PlaceName', 'StateName', 'ZipCode'))

issue_title: Support newer Attrs library
issue_body:
Currently, related can't be used alongside the current `pytest-cov` and `pytest-pep8` libraries. These require a more recent version of `attrs` (>= 17.2.0), whereas related is fixed at 17.1.0 -
 https://github.com/genomoncology/related/blob/master/setup.py#L30

```python
pluggy.PluginValidationError: Plugin 'pytest_cov' could not be loaded: (attrs 17.1.0 
...
, Requirement.parse('attrs>=17.2.0'), {'pytest'})!
```

Would it be possible to support and test also more recent versions of attrs in related?

So that's what i've done so far:

spotify = Spotify(self.soco)
spotify.add_track_to_queue(SpotifyTrack('spotify:track:7kCrYUDtWsPldohOKPTKPL'))

issue_title: Mock i18n functionality for text internal to the Perseus renderer

issue_title: Granting access to a user should not first revoke access
issue_body:
I noticed that granting model access to user is first calling the revoke method.
```
    async def grant(self, username, acl='read'):
        """Grant a user access to this model.
        :param str username: Username
        :param str acl: Access control ('read' or 'write')
        """
        controller_conn = await self.connection.controller()
        model_facade = client.ModelManagerFacade.from_connection(
            controller_conn)
        user = tag.user(username)
        model = tag.model(self.info.uuid)
        changes = client.ModifyModelAccess(acl, 'grant', model, user)
        await self.revoke(username)
        return await model_facade.ModifyModelAccess([changes])
```
The problem is when I created a user who has no modelaccess, this code fails because there is nothing to revoke.

```
2017-10-12 10:36:19,511 ERROR   File "/opt/sojobo_api/scripts/set_model_access.py", line 47, in set_model_acc
    await model.grant(user, acl='write')

    return self.gen.send(None)

issue_title: Add support for .fai FASTA index files
issue_body: We need a way to pull subjects out of a large FASTA file (ideally files, but let's just do one file for now). The FASTA index (fai) files that samtools makes give one way to do this. It's not optimal in some ways, but it's easy to implement, so let's try that. A better option, which allows the FASTA to remain compressed, would be the `BGZF` format. It's not simple to see how to work all these possible options into one FASTA reads class. For now do the simplest thing and keep moving on the actual work.

issue_title: Flowspec TCP Flags bit-match and exact-match
issue_body:
Juniper flowspec parses "tcp-flags syn" as bit-match and "tcp-flags =syn" as an exact-match. Exabgp only does exact match even without = (equals). Please consider allowing for bit matches?

[edit] set routing-options flow route test match tcp-flags syn
\# show route table inetflow.0 
\*,\*,tcp-flag:02/term:1 <== Matches any packet with SYN flag regardless of any other flags

[edit] set routing-options flow route test match tcp-flags =syn
\# show route table inetflow.0 
\*,\*,tcp-flag=02/term:1 <== Matches packets with SYN flag only.

Before opening this issue could you please:

For us to help you the outpout of exabgp -d is often required.
Please use ``` for any copied output.
-->

##### Summary
<!---
Explain the problem briefly
-->

Hello Guys,

my intention is to integrate exabgp into my own python program. So i do not want to make use of the process functionality. I integrate it as subprocess:
```
   self.exabgp_proc = subprocess.Popen(['exabgp','--env' ,'resources/exabgp_env.env' ,'-d'
                                ,'resources/exabgp_configuration.ini'],
                                stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
                                stderr=subprocess.STDOUT)
```
This makes me able to read from exabps stdout. My initial start for testing was to instruct a Thread to continiously search for Updates in stdout by using this function:
```
    def exabgp_output_parser(self):
        while True:
            try:
                output = self.exabgp_proc.stdout.readline()
                if output == '' and self.exabgp_proc.poll() is not None:
                    break
                if output:
                    # REGEX for BGP LS UPDATES
                    update = re.search("((?<=UPDATE.#\d.nlri..\(...2\).).*)", output.strip().decode("utf-8"))

issue_title: VI_NULL should be used as the first parameter to GetErrorMessage
issue_body:
Lines 232-235 of the generated session.py file are:
`           
error_code = buffer_size
buffer_size = self.library.niDMM_GetErrorMessage(self.vi, error_code, 0, None)
error_message = ctypes.create_string_buffer(buffer_size)
self.library.niDMM_GetErrorMessage(self.vi, error_code, buffer_size, ctypes.cast(error_message, ctypes.POINTER(ctypes_types.ViChar_ctype)))
`

The first parameter to each call of GetErrorMessage should be VI_NULL (or the Python equivalent).
If the session handle is invalid, GetErrorMessage will return a fatal status, which would then be used as the buffer size.

issue_title: Flesh out static parts of the documentation
get_property_datetime() returns wrong UTC
issue_body:
Hi,
I think there's a mistake in get_property_datetime() with the conversion to UTC:
```
print xmp.get_property(consts.XMP_NS_XMP, 'ModifyDate')
2012-03-21T08:57:17-07:00
print xmp.get_property_datetime(consts.XMP_NS_XMP, 'ModifyDate')
2012-03-21 01:57:17+00:00
```
but UTC of 08:57-07:00 (seven hours behind UTC) is 15:57+00:00. Patch:
```
--- a/libxmp/exempi.py
+++ b/libxmp/exempi.py
@@ -769,7 +769,7 @@
                                minutes=xmp_date_time.tzminute,
                                microseconds=xmp_date_time.nanosecond * 1000)
 
-    if xmp_date_time.tzsign < 0:
+    if xmp_date_time.tzsign > 0:
         the_date = utc_date - delta
     else:
         the_date = utc_date + delta
```
Best,
DS

issue_title: Publish docs to RTD
Process-to-WAMP example
issue_body:
I've been asked whether we could add an example how to communicate with a forked process via WAMP - eg expose the functionality of an executable that is then started dynamically.

There are 2 scenarios here:

1. the executable should be started, left running and communicated with over pipes. A WAMP session then exposes eg a procedure, and when that is called, some communication over (the existing) pipes happens
2. the executable should be started "one shot", that is, upon an incoming call to a WAMP registered procedure, the executable should be forked (presumably with command line args and/or stdin derived from the WAMP call parameters) and waited for exit.

This could reside as a new example here: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/examples/twisted/wamp/app/process2wamp

issue_title: Parse the log file for _actual_ parameters used to save in the model archive
issue_body: Instead of copying the input parameter file when archiving a model, we should parse the log file to get the actual parameters that were used (including defaults).  This will make model archiving more robust to changes in default parameters (as recently happened with the tokenizer).  Seems like quite a bit of work to be sure the parameters are parsed out correctly, though, and it's not super high priority.

Therefore, I wonder if you can officially upgrade the bioformats version that pims downloads/uses?

2) When .oir files reach a size larger than 1Gb, Olympus automatically begins splitting the file into subfiles (i.e. 'datafile.oir' will contain time points 0 through 399, then datafile_00001 will contain time points 400 through 799, etc...). 347             self._metadata = loci.formats.MetadataTools.createOMEXMLMetadata()

issue_title: Deis elements should use etcd2's permission resources
issue_body: The problem is still with us, that all etcd can be read by any container. We have already talked about defending strategies by using certificates (#3201) and/or using firewall, but I think the best way could be using [etcd2's permission resources](https://github.com/coreos/etcd/blob/master/Documentation/security_api.md). Yes, first it has to come to CoreOS stable channel, but it is possible to prepare :) I think there should be a `deis user` used by only `deis-*` containers and the `/deis` folder could be read only by this user.  I think this is a pretty easy solution to defend sensitive data stored in etcd (like applications specific private ssl keys).

issue_title: Improve `requests` error wrapping to provide a more controlled surface for our package
issue_body:
We've seen `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError` come back to use with a body of `('Connection aborted.', BadStatusLine("''",))`. We then present that as a `GlobusConnectionError` to better encapsulate our usage of `requests` as an HTTP layer.

This is a confusing error from `httplib` being wrapped by `requests` being wrapped by us. It likely relates to a malformed response. The discussion on a [related, underlying issue](kennethreitz/requests#2364) in `requests` itself suggested the possibility of a proxy chopping off parts of encrypted data, and breaking decryption.

To make matters worse, on newer Pythons, `httplib` will potentially change, changing `requests` and therefore us. This is bad: people may try to rely on our error messages, and we should have more control.

So, let's should do something like the following:
- Let `GlobusNetworkError`s take a custom message and a contained exception
- Keep the underlying exception distinct -- don't pass its args back to our own exception class
  - Do we need to pass some of its args through?
  - Preserve the original traceback?

We may want to move the `RequestsException` handler out of our underlying caller and into a helper, just to avoid bulking up `BaseClient` too much.

issue_title: The wrap method in Query breaks return_models
issue_body:
Using the `wrap` method in `Query` breaks the `return_models` functionality of the `select` method.

```python
>>> q = Query().from_table(Rating).limit(1).wrap()
>>> q.select(return_models=True)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-14-afd37124e1a1> in <module>()
----> 1 q.select(return_models=True)

/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/querybuilder/query.py in select(self, return_models, nest, bypass_safe_limit, sql, sql_args)
   1555             # create models if needed
   1556             if return_models:
-> 1557                 model_class = self.tables[0].model
   1558                 new_rows = []
   1559                 for row in rows:

AttributeError: 'QueryTable' object has no attribute 'model'
```

It's possible to work around the issue by assigning the `model` attribute manually.

```python
>>> q = Query().from_table(Rating).limit(1).wrap()
>>> q.tables[0].model = Rating
>>> q.select(return_models=True)
[<Rating: Rating object>]
```

Using the  `Query` without wrapping it works as expected.

```python
>>> q = Query().from_table(Rating).limit(1)
>>> q.select(return_models=True)
[<Rating: Rating object>]
```

Thanks for the awesome library, it has saved me a lot of effort!

issue_title: Re-organize networking code for Trinity
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Currently, all of the networking code lives under `evm.p2p`.  The *long* term plan is to split the core DevP2P components from this code into a separate library (probably the existing `devp2p` python library).  To prepare for this, we should start drawing firmer lines between the different sections of code that currently live in this repository.

1. Py-EVM code: deals specifically with the EVM
2. DevP2P code: deals with the generic DevP2P protocol
3. Trinity code: deals with running an Ethereum node over the DevP2P protocol using the `py-evm` EVM.



### How can it be fixed

Perform the following code re-organization.  This should be done in multiple steps to keep the pull requests smaller.

1. Everything from `evm.p2p` to a new root level module `devp2p`
2. Move everything from the new `devp2p` module which is not *strictly* DevP2P protocol code to `trinity.p2p`
3. Find *any* imports in either of these sections of code which import from `evm.utils.*` and change them to use their own local utilities, **or** migrate the utility to `eth-utils` so it can be shared.

issue_title: Type hierarchies
issue_body:
Add to the tfs module, or perhaps make a new one, for modeling type hierarchies.

Since Python has multiple-inheritance, it might be possible to do this with metaclasses. I.e., each TDL type gets a Python class. But that might be more trouble than it's worth.

Type hierarchies are one requirement for doing subsumption checking (e.g. for VPM or SEM-I)

issue_title: DMRX creates <realpred> nodes without required pos field
issue_body:
Jacy has a predicate like `_te_adjunct_rel`, which PyDelphin converts to the following realpred:

```xml
<realpred lemma="te" sense="adjunct" />
```

Then dmrx._decode_pred returns `None` for `elem.get('pos')`, and Pred.realpred trips on the non-string value of the pos field.

See: http://lists.delph-in.net/archives/developers/2017/002631.html

issue_title: mkprof partially broken in v0.7.0
issue_body:
The `delphin mkprof` command is broken in v0.7.0 unless `--full` is used. This is due to a partial switch to the new TestSuite class but with an expectation of the old ItsdbProfile functionality.

The simplest fix is to revert `itsdb.make_skeleton()` to use the ItsdbProfile class, which is deprecated but still available until v1.0.0 (see #139).

issue_title: Specifications in conflict after creating frozen (exported) environment from file
issue_body:
Hello,

`pip freeze` command creates a requirements file specifying all versions of all packages that can be used later with `pip install -r`. This usually works for years on my test servers without any intervention.

I thought the similar is expected from conda with its `export` and `conda create --file` workflow. 

However, recently I got `The following specifications were found to be in conflict` error after few months of successful creations of environment on a test server.

I assume that conda export and create haven't specified everything or there must be some kind of untracked changes inside packages without bumping versions. I would expect this might happen with some custom not-well-maintained packages, but in my case the error happened with a quite well known packages:

```
The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - boost 1.60.0 py27_0
  - pyqt 4.11.4 py27_4 -> qt >=4.8.6,<5.0
  - qt 4.8.7 4
```

How can I fix this but mainly how can I avoid such issues in the future?

issue_title: Add support for userid placeholder in .condarc values
issue_body:
In Enterprise settings it is often necessary to manage an Anaconda / conda installation on systems that multiple users have access to.  The users userid (username) is typically the main token used to control what the user has access to.  By allowing .condarc entries to contain userid placeholders that are substituted with the current users userid, centralized setup and management of Enterprise systems can be simpler, faster and more secure.

As an example, the envs_dirs directive allows an admin to set the default location of envs to be the users home directory, but what if the admin needs to make the default location a non-home sub-directory and the path includes the user's userid.  If the system has a mounted drive called /dev and the users want their envs created in /dev/<userid>/envs then this could be accomplished with one entry in the system .condarc file.  Using this approach users on the system can freely create envs using the centrally managed install of Anaconda.  The envs are on an admin managed disk with plenty of space.  The user didn't have to setup a .condarc file in their home directory.

envs_dirs:
  - /dev/<userid>/envs

conda-build:
  root_dir: /dev/<userid>/build

channles:
  - http://server/<userid>/
  - file:///dev/<userid>/pkgs

issue_title: conda env prompt
issue_body:
activate.bat change the PROMPT to show activated conda env, which is nice. However, it has conflict with other software that also tries to set PROMPT, for example, git use prompt to show current branch.

I found on google group the discussion about this:
https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!topic/conda/C4ll3MeLku0

which suggest an option to not change the PROMPT. 

However if I just change the following line:

set PROMPT=[%CONDA_NEW_NAME%] $P$G
to:
set PROMPT=[%CONDA_NEW_NAME%] %PROMPT%

It adds conda evn while keeping the PROMPT set by git.

I did not test other scenarios.

issue_title: conda install from GH sub-tree
issue_body:
E.g. Imagine the `usermgmt` directory (and its sub-content) in our original Wakari repo contained everything required for a conda package.  It would be great to be able to do:

``` bash
conda install https://github.com/ContinuumIO/wakari/tree/master/usermgmt
```

Nothing specific to an "app" in there, just need conda install to know how to handle URLs to GH, and act on a directory sub-tree instead of a repo.

issue_title: conda-env 2.6.0 for Linux can't import yaml
issue_body:
Hi!

I'm preparing a presentation on Conda for the Barcelona sysadmin community, and I found that latest conda-env=2.6.0 on Linux is broken. I can't export or use it in any way, since it can't import yaml, even when it should be there installed by conda setup.

I managed to workaround this using Miniconda, 3 and 2, 4.1.11, but when I get to install conda-build, it upgrades to the latest conda-env, no matter what I do, so I can't use it anymore. I have other issues with conda-build, but I'll create another issue there.

I've tried this on Ubuntu Trusty 64 and Xenial 64 vagrant boxes on VirtualBox.

Example conda info:
```
vagrant@vagrant-ubuntu-trusty-64:~$ conda info
Current conda install:

               platform : linux-64
          conda version : 4.2.11
       conda is private : False
      conda-env version : 4.2.11
    conda-build version : not installed
         python version : 2.7.12.final.0
       requests version : 2.11.1
       root environment : /home/vagrant/miniconda2  (writable)
    default environment : /home/vagrant/miniconda2
       envs directories : /home/vagrant/miniconda2/envs
          package cache : /home/vagrant/miniconda2/pkgs
           channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch
            config file : None
           offline mode : False
```

Error I get using conda env export:
```
vagrant@vagrant-ubuntu-trusty-64:~$ conda env export -n my-env > my-env.yml
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/vagrant/miniconda2/bin/conda-env", line 4, in <module>
    import conda_env.cli.main
  File "/home/vagrant/miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda_env/cli/main.py", line 35, in <module>
    from . import main_attach
  File "/home/vagrant/miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda_env/cli/main_attach.py", line 4, in <module>
    from ..env import from_environment
  File "/home/vagrant/miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda_env/env.py", line 17, in <module>
    from . import yaml
  File "/home/vagrant/miniconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda_env/yaml.py", line 8, in <module>
    import yaml
ImportError: No module named yaml
```
