issue_title: Singular inertia matrix on simple 90-degree rotation
issue_body:
We're seeing singular inertia matricies in a bunch of cases with exact 90-degree rotations (but not for small perturbations of those angles).

Small script which reproduces the failure

```
import math
from mujoco_py import load_model_from_xml, MjSim

xml = '''
<mujoco>
    <compiler angle="radian"/>
    <worldbody>
        <body>
            <joint axis="1 0 0" type="slide" />
            <joint axis="1 0 0" type="hinge" />
            <joint axis="0 1 0" type="hinge" name="y" />
            <joint axis="0 0 1" type="hinge" />
            <geom size="1" type="sphere" />
        </body>
    </worldbody>
</mujoco>
'''


def try_sim(r):
    sim = MjSim(load_model_from_xml(xml))
    sim.data.set_joint_qpos('y', r)
    sim.forward()


try_sim(math.pi / 2 + 1e-8)  # <- succeeds
try_sim(math.pi / 2 - 1e-8)  # <- succeeds
try_sim(math.pi / 2)  # <- fails
```

I'm going to try reproducing with mujoco (no python) but my hunch is this is a bug in mujoco, and not our module.

issue_title: How to use Graphene with Django REST Framework authentication
issue_body:
I got some REST API endpoints in Django and I wanted to use the same authentication for Graphene. The documentation does not provides any guidance.

I have `authentication_classes = (TokenAuthentication,)` in my API views. This was my solution:

urls.py:
```
# ...
from rest_framework.authentication import TokenAuthentication
from rest_framework.permissions import IsAuthenticated
from rest_framework.decorators import authentication_classes, permission_classes, api_view

def graphql_token_view():
    view = GraphQLView.as_view(schema=schema)
    view = permission_classes((IsAuthenticated,))(view)
    view = authentication_classes((TokenAuthentication,))(view)
    view = api_view(['POST'])(view)
    return view

urlpatterns = [
# ...
    url(r'^graphql_token', graphql_token_view()),
    url(r'^graphql', csrf_exempt(GraphQLView.as_view(schema=schema))),
    url(r'^graphiql', include('django_graphiql.urls')),
# ...
```

Note that I added a new `^graphql_token` endpoint and kept the original `^graphql` which is used by the GraphiQL tool.

Then, I set the `Authorization` header in my GraphQL client and point to the `graphql_token` endpoint.

I created this issue in case anyone else has the same question. Also, [posted the question and answer to StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/39026831/how-to-use-graphene-graphql-framework-with-django-rest-framework-authentication/39026832#39026832).

issue_title: Add error/warning for returning wrong types/fields
issue_body:
It has happened to me twice now that I had a situation similar to this:

``` py
class DirectDebitInstructionStatus(graphene.Enum):
    some = 1
    fields = 2
    ...

class DirectDebitInstruction(graphene.ObjectType):
    has = ...
    other = ...
    accepted_fields = ...

class someOtherNode(relay.Node):

    direct_debit_instruction = graphene.Field(DirectDebitInstruction)

def resolve_direct_debit_instruction(...):
    return DirectDebitInstructionStatus(has='something', other='something_else')
```

And on the frontend this just returns fields set to null. It would be great if there was either an exception thrown or at the very least some warning. I suppose that could be on two levels, one on the `graphql-core` and the other on `graphene`. `graphql-core` could simply warn/error if any fields are returned in the resolve that are not specified on the ObjectType/Node (Might take a look to see if `graphql-js` does anything there or has an issue related to that). `graphene` could even go a step further and could just throw/log if and `ObjectType` gets initialized with fields not defined in the class as well as when the wrong class gets returned.

So in this case that would be, 

for graphql-core: `Returning fields 'has', 'other' are not defined for type`DirectDebitInstruction`

for graphene: `DirectDebitInstructionStatus does not have fields 'has', 'other'` and `Returning type`DirectDebitInstructionStatus`when type`DirectDebitInstruction`was expected`

or along those lines.

What do you think @syrusakbary?

issue_title: Monitoring in Graphene Middleware
issue_body:
Apologies if this is a bit of a rookie question, but I'm unfamiliar with Graphene's middleware system and its reliance on promises, and attempting to make them play nicely with existing Python concepts.

I'd like to wrap Graphene operations with monitoring. What I'd like to do is something like...

```python
class MonitoringMiddleware(object):
    def resolve(self, next_, obj, info, **kwargs):
        with metrics.monitor():
            return next_(obj, info, **kwargs)
```

However, if I do this, I get unexpected or empty results. For example, our monitoring for number of database queries made is 0, even though data is returned. I think the reason for this is that the `next_` function resolves to a promise, so no work is actually done inside the `metrics.monitor` context manager.

I'd appreciate any help or feedback on how best to structure this. Is there anything simple that can be done here to wrap the execution of the promise – I need to do work before and after because it the monitoring needs to know initial and end states, and I also need to guarantee that the monitoring is called at the end to cleanup any mutated state.

The monitoring function is roughly:

```python
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def monitor():
    setup()
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        teardown()
```

Side note: I debated posting this on https://github.com/syrusakbary/promise, but decided to post it here as I think the solution might end up being a bit more tied to the middleware structure. Happy to repost on that repo if people think it appropriate.

It seems that deprecation of fields is not supported either in graphene or graphql-core. It seems to be explained here: https://github.com/facebook/graphql/blob/d8a52f1fb70a61ceb13dd49292b74b58d894a074/spec/Section%204%20--%20Introspection.md#deprecation

I would expect to be able to use it as follows:

```py
class User(relay.Node):
    billing_address = graphene.String(
        isDeprecated=True,
        deprecationReason='Selecting your billing address is not supported anymore',
        description='Selected billing address.')
```

issue_title: Incorrectly formatted error message.
issue_body:
The error message generated here is incorrect. It should be `only_type._meta.name` instead of `graphene_type._meta.name`. This way the expected value will be reported, which is what the message says it is.

https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/7bd77a0817677656e2ed8e8ac235ab5e8d557487/graphene/relay/node.py#L105

Also, it's better to use "a" instead of "an" as a default preposition in messages. https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/7bd77a0817677656e2ed8e8ac235ab5e8d557487/graphene/relay/node.py#L104

issue_title: I think the model converter is handling "null" incorrectly
issue_body:
I think that the converter code is generating the "required" param incorrectly.  It is basing it on Django's "null" attribute, rather than the "blank" attribute.

For example here: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-django/blob/master/graphene_django/converter.py#L121

[Documentation][1] for "blank" suggests that that is the one that should be used for validation, whereas "null" just refers to whether null values should be converted to empty strings or not.

[1]: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.Field.blank

I have a patch for this that I can send if you are interested.

Thanks

Ed

issue_title: Changes to support building in IDEs
issue_body:
**Highlevel: #1** - Current autoconf, ./configure, Makefile.am, etc (collectively, auto-build-system) method of building things is not very compatible with GUI based IDEs

**Highlevel #2** - Support for non-GNU toolchains

**Highlevel #3** - Data structures across applications and libraries must be identical at link time.

**Problem:** In src/core/Makefile.am - lists numerous IF/ELSE/ENDIF which choose which files are added or not added to a specific library.  While IDEs can provide some level of exclusion, they are not that flexible. 

_Note:_ There are many other examples in other Makefiles through out openthread.

**Solution:** Always include all source files in all builds, but in the source code use #IF/#ELSE/#ENDIF constructs to disable entire files. In some cases this will result in the compiler producing an "empty-translation unit"

_Note:_  Some IDEs support exclusion based on 'configuration name', but each IDE does this a different way

**Problem:**  Need both --enable-cli-ftd --enable-cli-mtd, and likewise  --enable-mtd 

Need granularity such that  CLI can be built with MTD only,, And NCP can be built using MTD or FTD
Current ./configure solution is:  FTD for both, or FTD for Neither

**Solution:**  Add more ./configure switches.

Add --enable-cli-{yes|mtd|ftd|no} where YES = both
Add: --enable-ncp-mode={mtd|ftd|both}
Note that: --enable-ncp={spi|uart} is already taken.

**Problem:** Libraries that include 'downstream' header files should be built using the same flavor of downstream header files the libraries they are are linked against where built with.

_Example:_ The NCP-MTD-APP - linked with the 'agnostic-NCP- library, linked with the Openthread-MTD library fails both (1) whole program optimization and (2) static code analysis checks because:  The NCP-Library was built against only one of the two (MTD or FTD) header file configurations.

**Solution:** Require all libraries and all applications to affirmatively choose MTD vrs FTD at build time such that libraries, and thus internal headers are consistent across the entire build.

**Problem:** Some toolchains do not provide all functions or have incompatible implementations.

Some of this was addressed previously (header files only), but not completely (source implementations of missing functions)

_Examples include:_  "strnlen()" - Spinel code has s a private copy of strnlen()

**Solution:** 

Must be: "IDE Friendly"
Add:  include/openthread/platform/missing.h - for missing prototypes.
Add:  Missing functions to platform specific code.
FUTURE: Perhaps these could be moved to a more common location.

issue_title: for v.11.3, proto_version accepts incompatible protobuf versions

The newly released v.11.3 specifies a proto_version range in python of 
lower: '3.0.0'
upper: '4.0dev'

If I am I reading this right, this specifies the acceptable range of versions for the protobuf gem. If so,  this range accepts now-incompatible versions. In particular, v.11.3 relies on calls to RegisterServiceDescriptor, which is not introduced in protobuf until version 3.3.x

This appears to be causing issues on Google Cloud ML Engine because the iam and pubsub libraries, at least in my case, are loading too early a version of protobuf and then blowing up when RegisterServiceDescriptor is called. This issue first appeared right after v.11.3 was released on the 14th.

BigchainDB.transactions.create()

issue_title: Problem: Some users unable to upgrade to the latest version
issue_body:
The latest version of the Python driver, 0.5.0a1, is a "pre-release" (or at least that's what I heard; I couldn't find any indication of this fact on PyPI, i.e. at https://pypi.python.org/pypi/bigchaindb-driver ). That means that just doing:
```bash
$ pip install -U bigchaindb_driver
```

will not upgrade them to 0.5.0a1, because it's not a "release", it's a pre-release. They must do:
```bash
$ pip install bigchaindb_driver==0.5.0a1
```

### To Do

- Figure out how to get PyPI to indicate that it's a pre-release.
- Update the docs (including those on PyPI) on how to install / upgrade the driver, to account for the case when the latest version is a pre-release.

issue_title: Joblib, parallel_backend, and performance
issue_body:
hi ya'll - Matthew suggested I ask some questions here. 

I'm a little confused on some results I'm seeing - and am wondering if you guys can help me figure out why I am seemingly executing grid searches in serial.

I modified dask-docker and am using containers on a docker swarm with 64GB RAM and 48 cores each.

```
search = RandomizedSearchCV(self.estimator, self.estimator.param_space, cv=3, n_iter=20, verbose=10)

if self.estimator.use_dask:

    address = 'tcp://dask-cluster:8786'
    c = Client(address)

    with parallel_backend('dask.distributed', scheduler_host=address,
                          scatter=[X.values, y.values]):
            search.fit(X.values, y.values)
```

where `X` is `pd.DataFrame` and y is `pd.Series`.

When I run the above with a small number of rows, I can see in the status page that lots of tasks get executed in parallel. The blocks of work on the task stream end up looking like a straight vertical since they all get dispatched nearly simultaneously.

Once I start increasing the number of rows, I seem to get more and more serial execution, where the status page shows that really one additional task gets added at a time. 

In this screenshot, I started at the full dataset, and started subtracting 10k on each run to see the affect on the execution time / parallelism. For some reason occasionally, e.g. on 80k and 40k, the work gets distributed a little differently?

![screen shot 2018-01-10 at 2 17 32 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1719730/34798532-5d67537e-f611-11e7-9fc3-0884e9a883dc.png)

When the number is higher, there is never more than one task active. When the number is lower, I see more (up to 4) getting triggered simultaneously.

Anyhow, my question ultimately is:

 - am I doing something in particular wong?
 - does this pattern look indicative of something I setup incorrectly?

issue_title: Email parser error message improvement
issue_body:
We came across some issues when getting Anymail set up with our project. Ended up figuring out that the issue was that we were setting `message.reply_to` to a string instead of a `list`.

Could be a minor improvement to either (1) automatically check if that variable is set to a string and automatically put it as a single-element list, or (2) throw a more helpful error message when any variable that should be a list of email addresses like `to, bcc, cc, reply_to` is the incorrect data type.

You can reproduce this by trying to send an email where `message.reply_to = "test@example.com"` instead of `message.reply_to = ["test@example.com"]`.

Minor little thing, but could prevent some issues in the future, since the resulting error message is pretty cryptic.

I realize this issue is pretty devoid of any specifics, so feel free to let me know if any more details would help.

issue_title: Error while calling overloaded functions
Static assertion in numpy.h fails when compiling with MINGW GCC
shared_ptr holder constructed but not freed by pybind11
issue_body:
I think I have found a problem with the construction/destruction of smart pointer holders. It seems like pybind11 does not always destruct the smart pointer holders it creates, because it thinks it doesn't "own" them.

Here is a small code snippet that demonstrates that behavior. It registers a class that uses ``std::shared_ptr`` as holder type. As suggested by the pybind11 documentation, ``std::enable_shared_from_this`` is used so that shared pointers and raw pointers can be used interchangeably.

```cpp
	class MyClass : public std::enable_shared_from_this<MyClass> {};
	py::class_<MyClass, std::shared_ptr<MyClass>>(m, "MyClass");

	std::shared_ptr<MyClass> instance = std::make_shared<MyClass>();	
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "1", that's the shared_ptr instance above.

	py::object o = py::cast(instance.get());  // Note we are passing a raw pointer to cast()
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "2", we now have a 2nd shared_ptr (the holder)

	o = py::none();	// Delete Python variable
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "2" (This seems wrong! The holder should have been destroyed)
```

Note that ``cast()`` assumes ``return_value_policy::reference``, because we are passing it a raw pointer. Even though a new ``shared_ptr`` holder is constructed by pybind11 as part of the cast, the ``wrapper->owned`` field is set to ``false`` by ``type_caster_generic::cast()``. This prevents the internal ``shared_ptr`` from getting freed when the wrapper is destroyed. A dangling reference to the C++ class instance remains.

The above problem can be worked around either by passing the ``shared_ptr`` to ``cast()``, not the raw pointer. Or by explicitly passing ``return_value_policy::take_ownership`` to ``cast()`` as second argument.

However, my C++ interface that I am trying to wrap with pybind11 typically returns raw pointers and relies on the ``std::enable_shared_from_this`` mechanism. This allows constructing reference counted pointers from raw pointers only when needed. That means I need ``cast()`` to behave the same for shared pointers and raw pointers. In both cases a ``shared_ptr`` holder should be constructed and, when the Python wrapper goes out of scope, destructed again.

issue_title: accessor::operator=() hides Python error
issue_body:
When doing something silly like `dict.attr("foo") = py::cast(my_object)` where `foo` doesn't exist, I'll get a `std::runtime_error("Unable to set object item");` which hides the underlying error from Python. Both [PyObject_SetAttr] and [PyObject_SetItem] raises an exception on failures.

[PyObject_SetAttr]: https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/object.html#c.PyObject_SetAttr
[PyObject_SetItem]: https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/object.html#c.PyObject_SetItem

issue_title: Documentation of the Eigen wrapper unclear
issue_body:
I'm a little confused by the documentation about the Eigen <-> Numpy conversion. In particular, the documentation has an example where the information is not propagated to the caller:

http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/master/advanced/cast/eigen.html

Why does the example not have the desired effect? As I understand, the Eigen and Numpy objects use the same memory buffer (via the buffer protocol). So although the Eigen object itself is not modified, the shared memory buffer should be.

Thank you for creating the pybind11 project!

issue_title: What is the correct way to bind copy constructor when class has Python`trampoline`?
issue_body:
Dear Pybind11 developers,

   I am trying to create bindings for copy-constructor for non-abstract class that have Python trampoline structure. What is the correct way to do that?

  For class `A` I have tried:
```cl.def( pybind11::init( [](A const &o){ return new A(o); }, [](A_trampoline const &o){ return new A_trampoline(o); } )```

 but that produce compilation error. So instead i just bind two 'copy-like' constructors:

```
cl.def( pybind11::init( [](A const &o){ return new A(o); } );
cl.def( pybind11::init( [](A_trampoline const &o){ return new A_trampoline(o); } )
```
  Is this the right way to do this?

Thanks,

issue_body: Implement an Authenticator plugin which can fulfill a dns-01 challenge using Lexicon.

issue_title: Find a way to interactively (not cli-only) modify the domains in a cert
issue_body: Perhaps `certbot edit --cert-name mycert` brings up a list of all domains it's found, with current domains highlighted? Or something?

issue_title: Zero protection on github - no signed code, no pgp/gpg, no certificates, horrifying dependency forest ...
issue_body: To get a SSL security certificate on my machine, I have to download unsigned and untrusted code from a public web site maintained by an unknown number of arbitrary contributors, and then run this code on my servers. Upon first-run, I'm greeted like this:- [sudo] password for gitcnd: ... this code then reaches out without warning or permission to assorted internet servers and gets even more software from dubious sources (yes, on centos/redhat, RPM has a signature-checking "feature", however, for the last 4 or 5 years since I filed a bug report on that [missing signatures are silently accepted!] those maintainers have chosen not to let RPM have any means to reject an "install request" because that might "break something" - they won't fix the bug, and they won't add that feature either). and who knows what happens next, because after many dozens of junk dependancies, something died and it all failed. This suggests to me that nobody in the letsencrypt project takes actual security even slightly seriously.

issue_title: Documentation: Developer's Guide does not mention steps needed to define a new plugin
issue_body:
https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html states:

> If you’re running on Linux, you can run the following commands to install dependencies and set up a virtual environment where you can run Certbot. You only need to do this once.
>
> ```
> cd certbot
> ./certbot-auto --os-packages-only
> ./tools/venv.sh
> ```

While this seems to be accurate for many (most?) cases, it seems to be necessary to re-run `./tools/venv.sh` when defining a new plugin in `setup.py`.

That may be an uncommon use case though, so perhaps that caveat belongs in the "Writing your own plugin" section. That section could also be expanded to describe the high-level changes which are necessary for defining a new plugin.

issue_title: Can you request a single cert with a wildcard and the base domain with our DNS plugins
issue_body:
There was some concern from @joohoi about whether or not we currently handle this correctly in any/all of our DNS plugins. @joohoi, if you could add some additional context here that'd be great. 

We should look into this issue soon as we're currently in the process of getting our DNS plugins packaged everywhere and we should try to avoid making distros immediately repackage them.

issue_title: Improve hooks error message
issue_body:
As reported by @joohoi in #3890:
>If the hook points to a actual script, which is not executable a somewhat uninformative error is shown to user:
>
>Unable to find --auth-hook command /tmp/non_executable.sh in the PATH.
>(PATH is /usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin)
>
>This is also a bit misleading, as having the script on $PATH isn't a requirement.

issue_title: Show expiration and renew dates in certbot renew output
issue_body:
When running `certbot renew` it shows following info (paths truncated):

`````
The following certs are not due for renewal yet:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem (skipped)
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem (skipped)
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem (skipped)
No renewals were attempted.
`````

It would be muchmore useful if it said when the certificates are going to expire and when they are due to renewal. For example:

`````
The following certs are not due for renewal yet:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem (skipped; exp. 2017-12-01, renew 2017-11-01)
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem (skipped; exp. 2017-12-05, renew 2017-11-05)
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem (skipped; exp. 2017-11-01, renew 2017-10-01)
No renewals were attempted.
`````

```
ConnectionError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /directory (Caused by NewConnectionError('<requests.packages.urllib3.connection.VerifiedHTTPSConnection object at 0x7f7f4f42f510>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno 110] Connection timed out',))

I'm fairly confident that this is due to it not using the https proxy set in both `https_proxy` and `HTTPS_PROXY` environment variables. Any ideas?

issue_title: Change all logger.warn("Fatal condition")s to logger.error
issue_body: In the current code, `logger.warning` is more common than `logger.error`, and often is in fact an error or imminently fatal condition. One day perhaps there should be clear semantics for the levels and those should be refactored to to `logger.error`.

issue_title: setup.sh should work when libscipopt and scip includes are installed in a standard location
issue_body:
I would suggest the following.
1) As seems to be standard with Cython extensions, users are expected to simply set CPPFLAGS and LDFLAGS so that the compiler will find headers ands the library.
2) Offer the SCIPOPTDIR variables as a convenience (which if provided would set include_dirs and library_dirs), but don't require it.
3) Don't require symlinks.

issue_title: ReplacementTransactionUnderpriced during fixture setup
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

During the cron job on Saturday there was a failure of the token network fixture setup. The entire log can be seen [here](https://gist.github.com/LefterisJP/fa0fa511cb12c833097b53f25e7f0156).

It seems that there were multiple calls to the deposit token transaction.

## Task

Find out why that could happen and implement a fix. If possible also reproduce this in a test.

## Possible cause

There can be a race between multiple `TokenNetwork` or `TokenNetworkRegistry` instances especially since we now have [RPC I/O](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/39c73c613d1e04fd53f1ad2f485bd39c8d9c476e/raiden/network/proxies/token_network.py#L72) inside the proxy constructors which introduces a context switch.

So it's possible that two different code paths race down to the `TokenNetwork` (or any other proxy constructor) and we end up making two different transactions with the same nonce which would manifest itself like the attached log with a `ReplacementTransactionUnderpriced`.

Using a lock would possibly fix this.

issue_title: Change secret expiration from uint64 to uint256
issue_body:
`expiration` is a block number and it should be as big as possible. There are no downsides of using `uint256`.

`TokenNetwork` contract already expects `uint256` when unpacking data for the batch unlock.

issue_title: Identical tasks cause clashes

issue_title: Add range filter in count/search/aggregate API
issue_body:
In gitlab by @sylvaingaudan on Jul 17, 2017, 17:03

Add `range` filter for count/search/aggregate operations.

A range is defined by:
- a field
- an inclusive or exclusive start
- an inclusive or exclusive end

If some ranges are defined on a same field, then an OR operator is defined between those ranges and only those ranges, AND otherwise.

issue_title: Retry policy when a worker crashes: a hook missing?
issue_body:
So currently if a worker crashes ray task running on that worker is automatically retried. Some things about that:
* I have not seen any way to control how many times this is retried? If my Python code calls into some C code which segfaults, I do not want it to be retrying all the time.
* How do I know as a caller that this happens? I have not seen anything about this available in `ray.get(...)`. I just see messages printed to my stdout as a caller, but not really something I can detect programmatically?
* This assumes that functions are pure or at least idempotent. But what if they are not? Then calling a function again might break things and get things into an undefined state (or into using an undefined state left over from the previous run).

So, maybe what is missing is a hook which is called once a worker dies. Which by default marks the task for retrying, but it can also do something else. Like cleanup broken outside state used by the task, or decide not to run and propagate an error to the caller. I would not mind even that `ray.get(...)` simply aborts with an exception saying "worker terminated", do whatever you want. But ideally, I would prefer to have a hook I can register to handle even task I am not `ray.get`-ting.

issue_title: When a remote function raises an exception, it should be easy to get its arguments and run the function locally.
issue_body: When a remote function throws an exception, we should provide a method for getting the arguments and name of the function that failed and make it easy to run the function locally.

issue_title: Should Result objects consume themselves when unwrapped?
issue_body:
It's generally a mistake to unwrap the same `Result` object twice, because if it's an exception you'll end up with a corrupted traceback as the same exception object gets raised in two unrelated call stacks.

We should at least document this, and maybe we should make `unwrap` "consume" the object so that if you call it twice then the second time it raises an error.

issue_title: Parsl internal bugs silently squashed then hangs
issue_body:
@benclifford reported an alarming bug in which a error in the parsl code does not throw an exception but is silently squashed. This is followed by the code hanging and not making progress. To make the situation even worse the strategy thread continues to run, giving the impression that parsl is doing something. 

My suspicion is the the exception silently kills some thread, and that goes unreported.

The string formatting bug added here (in memoization code) -> https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/tree/pr238-related-example reproduces this issue with a test script that hits this memoization code. 

Some recommendations:
1. Better exception handling
2. Logs to show thread-ids so that we can see when threads have gone missing

issue_title: Optionally load jep module using a explicit path
issue_body:
I have to launch JVMs from a bunch of different contexts and don't necessarily want to have to set `java.library.path` in all of those locations. For simplicity's sake I'd just like to set the location of the jep native module after my application has started. At the moment that means dangerously hacking the `java.library.path` variable via reflection, which is unreliable depending on the security context.

So, it would be nice to be able to load the jep native module using an explicitly defined path, supplied by a system property via `System.load`. If the system property doesn't exist, Jep can still load the module via the `java.library.path` value.

issue_title: JOSS Review
issue_body:
Hey @scls19fr I've taken a look at your submission, https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/268,
 and found most everything to be in order, with some minor comments. Overall, I appreciate the job you've done making installation easy and getting started very straightforward. I don't analyze wind data often but I think I would able to use Windrose pretty easily if I ever did.

I've re-visited the reviewer checklist, which was left in an incomplete state due to the last reviewer, and verified every checked item and either checked or unchecked the ones I had comments on. Below are my comments. I'd like to see you at least address my comments on versions and documentation prior to my acceptance.

> License: Does the repository contain a plain-text LICENSE file with the contents of an OSI approved software license?

I checked this because I saw the above conversation with @arfon and assumed that dual licensing was both okay and done in a suitable fashion.

> Version: Does the release version given match the GitHub release (1.6)?

No, version latest release, which addresses the previous licensing issues, is 1.6.3. 1.6.3 is also the version installed via pip and the version I tested the functionality of. We should update the JOSS submission and archive so its for the 1.6.3 release.

> Functionality documentation: Is the core functionality of the software documented to a satisfactory level (e.g. API method documentation)?

The documentation that is present is pretty good (especially the readme) but I think it could be more comprehensive, especially where completely absent and where `**kwargs` is used for parameters. The most complete documentation for this package was in the docstrings which I had to browse the source to read. I think a dedicated and more complete documentation site (such as those we often generate with readthedocs) would be super helpful for new and existing users. What do you think about getting something like this working, @scls19fr? You might consider trimming the readme a bit and putting the content contained there the aforementioned documentation. Alterantively, you may find it easier to just write more documentation into the readme, which would be fine.

> Automated tests: Are there automated tests or manual steps described so that the function of the software can be verified?

There are manual examples which may serve as tests and a basic set of automated functionality tests included with the package, though there were no unit or regression tests (which could be nice!). Since this is a plotting package, a large amount of the functionality, and therefore potential places for bugs in the user experience depends on matplotlib, I wonder what the author would think about including automated or semi-automated visual regression tests. An easy first pass at this could just involve setting a random seed at the top of the visual regression test suite, adding some tests which create the various kinds of plots the package produces, and diffing the output with version-controlled copies from the last test run.

> Software paper

I'll wait to comment on this until the author updates the paper as per the editor's notes.

> Other notes:

These are general comments and don't need to be addressed for the JOSS submission:

- Noticed some markdown headers in windrose_sample_random.ipynb are missing a space between the # and the header which prevents proper formatting
- Noticed the global, `RESOLUTION`, is not actually used. Might be good to lint the source to find similar issues.
- When I ran this code:

    ```py
    ax = WindroseAxes.from_ax()
    ax.contourf(wd, ws, bins=np.arange(0, 8, 1), cmap=cm.hot)
    ax.set_legend()
    ```

    I got this warning:

    > /Users/bryce/src/windrose/windrose/windrose.py:394: MatplotlibDeprecationWarning: The poly_between function was deprecated in version 2.2.
      xs, ys = poly_between(angles, 0, val)

issue_title: inconsistent use of range parameters in documentation
issue_body:
`date` as well as other keywords accepting data ranges (such as `cloudcoverpercentage`) have to be provided as tuples.

This is reflected in the [API docs](http://sentinelsat.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#sentinelsat.SentinelAPI.query) automatically generated by `numpydoc`.

Other parts of the doc still list the old notation (`cloudcoverpercentage = [0 to 30]`) and use the wrong date format and therefore do not work.

- [API Quickstart](http://sentinelsat.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#quickstart)
- [sorting&filtering example](http://sentinelsat.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#sorting-filtering)

Using tuples for range values should be reflected in all parts of the documentation.

issue_title: CoreML UnaryFunctionLayerParams.Operation Threshold converted wrong ONNX Clip
issue_body:
UnaryFunctionLayerParams.Operation Threshold is f(x) = max(α, x). (https://apple.github.io/coremltools/coremlspecification/sections/NeuralNetwork.html#unaryfunctionlayerparams-operation). That is to say, if the element of x < α，we use α. If the element of x > α, we use x. i.e. our min value is α 

But we convert it Clip() { attribute: max: α) (https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#Clip) , ONNX's meaning is:
> max : float
Maximum value, above which element is replaced by max

i.e. If the element of x > MaxValue (α)，we will use α. The meaning is opposite of CoreML's threshold function。CoreML‘s threshold meaning is that our min value is α, not max value is α.

issue_title: Contextually-Aware token generator
issue_body:
I'd like to propose a backwards-incompatible change -- adding a `context` keyword argument to `oauthlib.common.generate_token`.  (A backwards-compatible, but messier, version would be to specify and track multiple token generators).

Why?

This function is re-used multiple times throughout the library without regard to context, and context can be important.

The token generator is currently used in these situations:

oauth-1
- rfc5849-access_token
- rfc5849-access_token_secret
- rfc5849-request_token
- rfc5849-request_token_secret
- rfc5849-verifier

oauth-2
- rfc6749-authorization_code
- rfc6749-prepare_authorization_request
- oauth2-generate_client_id
- BearerToken-access_token
- BearerToken-refresh_token

utilities
- random_token_generator

When generating a custom token, it's not uncommon for providers to prefix/suffix a token with a public identifier for faster lookup.  If the token_generator were contextually aware, this feature would be possible in this library.

        ls = os.listdir(p)
        print "xxxinfo: ls = %s" %(ls)
        matched = False
        for f in ls:
            if f == 'setup.py' or egginfo_pattern.match(f):
                print "xxxinfo: f = %s" %(f)
                matched = True
                break
        if matched:
            break
        p_prev = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(p, '..'))
        print "xxxinfo: p_prev = %s" %(p_prev)
        if p == p_prev:
            return None
        p = p_prev
    return p


def get_project_root(test_case_filename):
    return os.getenv("PROJECT_ROOT") or detect_package_root(test_case_filename) or os.getcwd()


def has_override(method, subCls, baseCls):
    return getattr(subCls, method) != getattr(baseCls, method)

issue_title: generalize ${X-yD} parsing to work with a sarra interval.
issue_body:
There is some hard coded parsing that sees a substitution such as:

${YYYYMMDD-1D}  and manually substitutes subtracting 1 day (24*3600) from the current time to calculate the value.  Ideally the code would parse the value after the minus sign as a sarra interval,
so you could enter 1h, 3w, 1m or 4d and it would all be correctly dealt with.

This would enhance consistency with the rest of the configuration language.

issue_body: Hi, I am trying to write a parsing script for vsif/vsof to python objects but I am running into a small issue. I am using "<text>" /(.|\\n|\\r)*/ /(NEWLINE)*/ "</text>" in my grammar to store text tokens but it seems to have trouble when things are written in new lines. If the whole "<text>" /(.|\\n|\\r)*/ /(NEWLINE)*/ "</text>"  is written in a single line the grammar parsing correctly but when there are new lines I get: Expecting: {'__SEMICOLON', 'NON_RESERVED_CHARACTER_TOKEN', 'BLANK'}. Is there anything I can change to make this work? Thanks.

issue_title: RDFa parser fails on typed literals when xml:lang is specified
issue_body:
vfaronov, 2010-02-09T21:18:39.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Prepare an XHTML+RDFa document in which there are both an element
producing a typed literal, and an xml:lang specification (e.g. on the root
html element). An example document is attached.
2. Feed this file to the RDFLib parser.

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
The expected output is one triple in which the object is a typed literal,
as per "RDFa in XHTML: Syntax and Processing" section 6.3.1.2. Instead,
RDFLib reports "TypeError: A Literal can only have one of lang or datatype,
per http://www.w3.org/TR/rdf-concepts/#section-Graph-Literal"

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
I am using RDFLib trunk (r1843) on Debian GNU/Linux 5.0.

Please provide any additional information below.
The issue exists because the parser passes the current lang value to the
Literal() constructor even though the datatype is specified. The
constructor then complains.

Comment 1 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 2 by vfaronov
The attached patch fixes the issue for me without causing TC #108 to fail.


Comment 3 by eik...@gmail.com
Fixed in r1842. Thank you for reporting the issue and supplying the patch.

The next release is going to be the 3.0 release.


Comment 5 by vfaronov
eikeon, this issue has already been fixed in r1845. It just wasn't marked as fixed
for some reason.


Comment 6 by eik...@gmail.com
vfaronov, thank you. Marking as fixed now.
